amalfi.metaTitle: Amalfi Coast & Bay of Naples Itineraries | Boat4You
amalfi.metaDesc: Routes from Salerno, Sorrento and the Ligurian coast: Capri, Ischia, Procida and the Amalfi cliffs — with honest buoy-field advice.
amalfi.title: Italy's most dramatic mile of coastline
amalfi.description: This is theatre sailing. The Amalfi coast drops a thousand metres of lemon terraces straight into the sea, Capri's faraglioni stand in water so blue it looks lit from below, and Vesuvius watches the whole bay. It is not wilderness — summer brings ferry wakes, buoy fees and a queue for everything — but nothing else in the Mediterranean looks like this from a cockpit. The trick is timing: Capri at eight in the morning belongs to you; at noon it belongs to everyone. Procida and Ischia carry the quieter nights, and Salerno's Marina d'Arechi makes a calm, well-run start. Anchoring is restricted along much of the coast, so budget for buoys — and consider it the ticket price for the view.

sardinia.metaDesc: Routes from Olbia, Portisco and Cannigione: Costa Smeralda, the La Maddalena islands and Caribbean-clear water, day by day.
sardinia.title: Granite islands, impossible water
sardinia.description: North-east Sardinia is the Mediterranean doing an impression of the Caribbean and pulling it off. The La Maddalena archipelago is a maze of pink granite and turquoise shallows where every second cove looks photoshopped; Spargi and Budelli hold the famous ones, but the plain ones would be famous anywhere else. The Costa Smeralda adds the yachts-and-aperitivo end of the spectrum at Porto Cervo, worth one evening of people-watching. Distances are short, shelter is everywhere, and when the mistral pipes up the archipelago itself flattens the sea. You need a permit for the Maddalena park — sort it before the week, not at the ranger boat. Then go get lost in it; that's the whole idea.

sicily.metaDesc: Routes from Palermo, Capo d'Orlando and Portorosa through the Aeolians: Stromboli's glow, Salina's greens, Lipari's harbours.
sicily.title: Sail a chain of live volcanoes
sicily.description: The Aeolians are seven volcanoes standing in deep blue water, and a charter here feels closer to expedition than holiday — in the gentlest possible way. You'll swim over bubbles rising from the seabed off Panarea, eat granita in Lipari's pastel harbour, climb through Salina's vineyards, and finish with the one sight nobody forgets: Stromboli throwing red embers into the night sky while you watch from the cockpit a mile off. Anchorages are deep and shelves are narrow, so mooring takes attention; buoys and short quays fill early in August. Black-sand beaches, capers and malvasia wine — the islands feed you well between passages. Palermo and Portorosa starts both work; Capo d'Orlando gets you to Vulcano fastest.

capo-dorlando.metaDesc: Sail seven days from Capo d'Orlando through Vulcano, Salina, Stromboli, Panarea and Lipari — the volcanic Aeolian Islands off Sicily's north coast.

1.shortDescription: An easy 20-mile hop north to Vulcano, where the sulphur fumaroles and a stiff climb up La Fossa set the tone for the week.
1.description: The first leg is deliberately gentle, about twenty miles of open water to loosen the crew and the rig before the real island-hopping starts. Vulcano rises ahead long before you smell it; the sulphur reaches you a mile or two out. Porto di Levante on the east side is where most charter boats tie up, close enough to walk everything. The mud pool by the harbour foot is the island's calling card, a warm sulphurous soak that clings to your skin and your swimwear for days. Just offshore, gas vents bubble up through the shallows and warm the sea around your ankles. If the crater trail is open, the climb up La Fossa takes roughly an hour and pays out a full view of the archipelago. We usually save it for late afternoon, when the light softens and the heat lifts.
thingsToDo: Soak in the Laghetto dei Fanghi mud pool
Climb La Fossa crater for the panorama
Snorkel the bubbling vents off Porto di Levante
Salt-cod stew at a Levante quayside trattoria
1.mooringTip: Take a stern-to berth in Porto di Levante and pick a spot on the north side of the basin, upwind of the fumaroles, or the sulphur will follow you into the cockpit. It's well sheltered from the prevailing northerlies; expect roughly €60–100 a night in season.

2.shortDescription: Twenty miles west to Filicudi, the quietest island in the group, with the La Canna spire standing offshore like a broken tooth.
2.description: Filicudi trades nightlife for silence. A few hundred people live here, connected by mule tracks rather than roads, and the pace drops the moment you drop anchor. The landmark is La Canna, a slender volcanic needle rising better than eighty metres straight out of the sea a mile off the west coast; motor around it slowly and the crew will reach for their cameras. Below the waterline the rock walls fall away into clear blue, good snorkelling if the swell is down. The little port on the east side has a handful of berths and a couple of trattorie. For dinner ashore we prefer Pecorini a Mare on the south coast, a fishing hamlet of pastel houses where the sea-urchin pasta is worth the dinghy ride.
thingsToDo: Circle the La Canna sea stack under sail
Kayak into the Grotta del Bue Marino
Walk up to the Capo Graziano ruins
Ricci sea-urchin pasta at Pecorini a Mare
2.mooringTip: Filicudi Porto has stern-to space for a dozen boats and holds well against the northerly; get in by early afternoon in July or you'll be waiting for a gap. If the port is full, Pecorini a Mare is a decent anchorage on sand in five to seven metres, though it's open to the south.

3.shortDescription: A short eastward run to Salina, the green one, where caper terraces and Malvasia vines climb between two old volcanoes.
3.description: After two barren islands, Salina comes as a shock of green. Two extinct cones frame the island, and the saddle between them holds vineyards, caper terraces and citrus, all fed by springs the other Aeolians lack. Santa Marina on the east coast is the usual base, a tidy town strung along one long street of wine bars and gelato counters. The sweet Malvasia here is the island's signature; a tasting at one of the hillside cellars makes an easy afternoon. Rent a scooter and cross to Pollara on the far side, a half-drowned crater where a slice of the village stands above a black-pebble bay. Older crew will recognise it as the backdrop to Il Postino. The swimming below the cliffs is some of the best on the route.
thingsToDo: Taste Malvasia at a Salina hillside cellar
Swim the black-pebble bay at Pollara
Buy Salina capers in Santa Marina
Granita and pane cunzato at Lingua
3.mooringTip: Santa Marina Salina takes you stern-to on the town quay, sheltered from the north but rolly when the sirocco pushes swell onto the east coast. Book ahead in August; the quay is small and popular. Rinella, tucked under the south coast, is a calmer fallback if the wind swings east.

4.shortDescription: Twenty miles northeast to Stromboli, the volcano that never sleeps, timing the arrival so you're off the Sciara del Fuoco at dusk.
4.description: Stromboli is the reason many crews book this route. The cone throws up bursts of glowing rock at short intervals, day and night, and the show is best from the water after dark. The action falls down the Sciara del Fuoco, a scar of black scree on the northwest flank that runs straight into the sea. Plan the day so you reach that side around sunset, then hold station offshore while the mountain does its thing overhead. It's genuinely loud. During the day, tie up at Scari on the northeast and wander the black lanes of the village, or dinghy round to Ginostra, a car-free cluster of white houses reached through what may be the smallest harbour in Italy. Summit hikes run guided only, and access shifts with the volcano's mood, so check locally before planning one.
thingsToDo: Watch the Sciara del Fuoco erupt after dark
Dinghy to car-free Ginostra village
Pizza with a volcano view at L'Osservatorio
Wander the black lanes of San Vincenzo
4.mooringTip: There's no real marina on Stromboli. For the night show, anchor off the Sciara del Fuoco on rock and sand in eight to twelve metres and post an anchor watch, since it's an open roadstead. Only stay overnight in settled weather; if anything is forecast, take a berth at Scari or move on to Panarea.

5.shortDescription: A dozen miles down to Panarea, the small, stylish island, with a Bronze Age cove at one end and cocktail bars at the other.
5.description: Panarea is the smallest of the seven and by far the most polished. In July its little harbour fills with tenders ferrying people between yachts and the whitewashed lanes of San Pietro, where the boutiques and bars stay busy past midnight. Don't let the glamour distract from the archaeology. Off the south coast, Cala Junco is a near-perfect cove backed by the stone footings of a Bronze Age village on the headland above, and the snorkelling over the seagrass is lovely. Push a mile northeast to Basiluzzo and you can drift over Roman remains lying just below the surface. We tend to anchor at Cala Junco for the swim and the quiet, then move round to San Pietro if the crew wants the evening out.
thingsToDo: Snorkel Cala Junco below the Bronze Age huts
Drift over the Roman ruins off Basiluzzo
Swim the reddish sand at Cala degli Zimmari
Late drink at San Pietro's cliffside bars
5.mooringTip: San Pietro sits behind a field of pricey mooring buoys rather than a proper marina, and in peak season they book out and run steep. Reserve a buoy in advance or plan to anchor south at Cala Junco on sand in four to six metres and tender in. The whole east side is exposed if the wind backs to the east.

6.shortDescription: Twelve miles to Lipari, the busy capital, home to the archipelago's best museum and a swim beneath white pumice cliffs.
6.description: After days of quiet anchorages, Lipari brings back the bustle: the biggest island in the group, with proper shops and a real town. The old citadel sits on a rock above the harbour, and inside its walls the Aeolian archaeological museum is genuinely first-rate; give it a couple of hours for the painted Greek vases and the theatre masks pulled from the necropolis. Below the castle, the cobbled lanes lead down to Marina Corta and the Norman cathedral. Along the north coast the old pumice workings at Porticello leave the seabed pale and the water an odd, luminous turquoise, and swimming there feels faintly unreal. Provision well here before the run back to Sicily; this is the last decent supermarket on the route.
thingsToDo: Tour the Aeolian archaeological museum in the castle
Swim the white pumice shallows at Porticello
Climb to the Norman cathedral and citadel
Rabbit cunigghiu stew near Marina Corta
6.mooringTip: Marina Lunga on the north side of town takes yachts stern-to and has a fuel berth by the entrance, which makes it the natural place to top up before the return leg. Rates climb toward €70–120 in high summer. It's sheltered from the northerly but takes a swell from the east, so lines and fenders earn their keep.

7.shortDescription: The final twenty miles south to Sicily, with a last swim in a quiet bay before you hand the boat back at Capo d'Orlando.
7.description: The last day is a straight reach back across to the Sicilian shore, roughly twenty miles with the islands falling away astern. Leave in good time and there's usually a window before the afternoon breeze fills in. We like to break the crossing with a swim in a sheltered bay along the coast, a last dip in cool water while someone rinses the salt off the deck. Aim to be alongside at Capo d'Orlando Marina by early afternoon, which leaves the evening for refuelling, a proper clean and packing without a rush. The town itself is worth a wander once the boat's squared away: a ruined castle on the headland, a long seafront promenade, and almond granita from the kiosks that is exactly what you want after a week in the sun. Confirm your handover slot the day before so the morning goes smoothly.
thingsToDo: Last swim in a bay along the coast
Almond granita from a Capo d'Orlando kiosk
Walk the headland to the ruined castle
Crew dinner on the Lungomare promenade
7.mooringTip: Capo d'Orlando Marina berths you stern-to on lazy lines, with the fuel berth by the entrance, so refuel on the way in. Peak-season rates run around €70–110. Call ahead to confirm your handover time; arriving by early afternoon gives you room to fuel, wash down and settle up before dark.

genova.metaDesc: A week's yacht charter from Genoa west along the Riviera di Ponente through Arenzano, Varazze, Savona, Albenga and Imperia, then home past the Lanterna.

1.shortDescription: A gentle 12-mile warm-up west of Genoa to shake down the boat, ending stern-to at Arenzano beneath the Beigua hills.
1.description: The first leg is short on purpose. You collect the boat at Marina Porto Antico, run through the systems, then slip out past the Lanterna and turn west. Twelve miles of open coast, the Beigua ridge rising green behind the shore. In July the morning stays glassy; the libeccio fills in from the southwest by early afternoon, usually ten to fifteen knots, so we like to be off the dock by ten and let the breeze do the work on the way in. Arenzano itself is unhurried. The old resort villas sit back from a pebble beach, and behind the seafront the Parco Villa Negrotto Cambiaso spreads out with its little lake and aviary. Grab focaccia while the crew settles in. It is a soft start, which is exactly what a first day should be.
thingsToDo: Wander the Parco Villa Negrotto Cambiaso gardens
Swim off the Spiaggia Libera pebbles
Focaccia al formaggio from a village bakery
Hike a ridge trail in Beigua Park
1.mooringTip: Marina di Arenzano takes you stern-to and shelters well from the north; reckon on €60–100 a night in season. If you want more room for the handover, Genoa's Marina Porto Antico is the bigger base to leave from.

2.shortDescription: Barely eight miles down the coast to Varazze, one of the Riviera's biggest marinas, with room to breathe and sea glass on the beach.
2.description: This is the shortest hop of the week, so there is no rush. Sleep in, top up water, and make the eight miles at your own pace. Marina di Varazze is one of the largest yards on the Riviera di Ponente, around eight hundred berths, lazy lines on every finger and a fuel dock if you need it. Slot capacity is rarely a problem here, even in August. The town behind is honest, working Liguria rather than a postcard. Old men play cards along the Lungomare Europa, the promenade laid over a disused railway that runs on toward Cogoleto. Kids comb Spiaggia di Ponente for sea glass. Eat fritto misto from a paper cone and walk it off along the front.
thingsToDo: Hunt sea glass on Spiaggia di Ponente
Snorkel the reef off Capo Santo Stefano
Fritto misto from a paper cone
Stroll the Lungomare Europa promenade
2.mooringTip: Marina di Varazze berths you stern-to on lazy lines and is fully sheltered; peak nights run €70–120. There is almost always space, so no need to book far ahead.

3.shortDescription: Another easy eight miles to Savona, a real working port crowned by the Priamar fortress and threaded with Columbus history.
3.description: Savona is a proper city, not a resort, and the better for it. The approach is unmistakable: the great bulk of the Priamar fortress on its headland, sixteenth-century stone thrown up by the Genoese, with the striped Torre Leon Pancaldo marking the old harbour mouth. Tie up in the Old Port marina, fully sheltered, and you step straight into the town. Columbus knew these streets; his family lived here for a spell. Climb the fortress walls for the view over the roofs, then find salt-cured anchovies and a glass of Vermentino somewhere off the main drag. If the marina is full, Vado Ligure sits three miles south with quieter berths.
thingsToDo: Walk the sixteenth-century Priamar fortress walls
Swim the Mermaid's Grotto cove south of port
Salt-cured anchovies at a backstreet osteria
A glass of local Vermentino ashore
3.mooringTip: Savona's Old Port marina puts you stern-to and fully sheltered, around €70–110 a night. Quieter Vado Ligure is three miles south if you would rather skip the city bustle.

4.shortDescription: The week's first real passage, twenty miles west to Albenga, a Roman town of brick towers with Gallinara island guarding the bay.
4.description: Twenty miles today, the longest sail so far, so we leave by nine to carry the morning calm and pick up the sea breeze as it fills. Off to port the coast opens into the Albenga plain, flat and green, unusual on this cliff-bound Riviera. The old town sits a little inland, a knot of brick towers left from the Middle Ages, and at its heart the fifth-century Battistero Paleocristiano still holds its original mosaics. Isola Gallinara rises just off the bay, a humped nature reserve with clear water on its lee side, worth an anchor and a snorkel before you go in. Ashore, this is Pigato country. Order a plate of buridda under one of the medieval towers and pour the local white.
thingsToDo: See the fifth-century Battistero Paleocristiano mosaics
Anchor and snorkel off Isola Gallinara
Buridda fish stew under a medieval tower
Pigato wine from the Albenga plain
4.mooringTip: Marina di Albenga sits you stern-to and shelters from the north, around €50–80 a night. For a lunch stop, anchor on the sand in the lee of Isola Gallinara and dinghy the crew ashore.

5.shortDescription: Fifteen miles on to Imperia, Liguria's olive-oil capital, where the hilltop Parasio looks down over two old towns and one marina.
5.description: Imperia is really two towns that were married off in the 1920s, Oneglia and Porto Maurizio, sitting either side of a small river with the marina in the middle. The fifteen-mile run down from Albenga is straightforward, a beam reach on the afternoon breeze most days. Porto Maurizio is the one to walk. Climb into the Parasio, the old quarter stacked up the hill in tight lanes, and look back down at the boats. Over in Oneglia, the Museo dell'Olivo tells the story of the taggiasca olive, the small dark fruit behind Liguria's best oil. Buy a tin to take home. Dinner is sardenaira, the local onion-and-anchovy focaccia, eaten near the port.
thingsToDo: Climb the hilltop Parasio in Porto Maurizio
Museo dell'Olivo and the taggiasca story
Swim the golden sand at Spiaggia d'Oro
Sardenaira near the Oneglia port
5.mooringTip: Marina di Imperia berths you stern-to between the two halves of town, fully sheltered, €80–130 in peak. Slot capacity holds up even in August, so a same-day call usually lands a berth.

6.shortDescription: The long turn back east, twenty-four miles to the working port of Vado Ligure, with the Bergeggi caves and a coastal bike path nearby.
6.description: This is the big mileage day, twenty-four back east, so an early line-off pays. Take the whole coast in reverse: Imperia falling astern, then the long green run past Alassio and Finale toward Savona's roadstead. On a good afternoon the breeze sits behind you and it is a fast, easy reach. Vado Ligure is no one's postcard. It is a real port of fishing boats and cargo cranes, and the yacht berths are almost an afterthought, which keeps them cheap and calm. Before you tie up, it is worth a detour to the Bergeggi marine reserve just west of the harbour, where the tender can nose into sea caves for a snorkel. Ashore, hire a bike and roll along the seafront, then eat plain, good seafood at a family-run trattoria near the fish quay.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the Bergeggi marine reserve caves
Anchor swim at Baia dei Saraceni
Hire a bike along the seafront
Fresh seafood by the Vado fish quay
6.mooringTip: Vado Ligure takes you stern-to for a modest €40–60 a night and shelters from the north. If it feels too industrial, Marina di Savona is only three miles north with more comforts.

7.shortDescription: The last leg east, twenty miles home to Genoa, pausing at little Cogoleto before you raise the Lanterna and hand the boat back.
7.description: The final run is an easy twenty miles, and there is no reason to hurry it. Break the leg at Cogoleto, a low-key seaside town that likes to claim a thread of the Columbus family story. Anchor off and swim beneath the old Punta Martinetto watchtower, or just drift for an hour before the last push. Back at Genoa the Lanterna comes up first, the medieval lighthouse that has guided crews in for centuries. Top up fuel at the entrance berth before you turn into Marina Porto Antico. There is time, if you are quick with the lines, to lose yourself once more in the caruggi, the old town's shadowed alleys, and eat pesto the way it was meant to taste, in the city that invented it.
thingsToDo: Swim below the Punta Martinetto watchtower
Refuel and moor at Marina Porto Antico
Pesto genovese in the caruggi alleys
See the medieval Lanterna lighthouse
7.mooringTip: Marina Porto Antico in the heart of Genoa berths you stern-to, €100–150 on peak nights and sheltered from the north. Hit the fuel berth at the harbour entrance before you moor, and give them your return time in advance.

lavagna.metaDesc: A 7-day sail from Lavagna down the Ligurian Riviera: Sestri Levante, Bonassola, the Cinque Terre and the Portofino marine reserve.

1.shortDescription: A gentle four-mile warm-up south from Lavagna's vast marina to Sestri Levante, where two bays sit back to back on one slate headland.
1.description: Marina di Lavagna is a small city of masts, so give yourself time to clear the fairway before the short run south. Sestri Levante sits on a stubby peninsula with a bay on either flank. Baia del Silenzio, the eastern one, is the postcard: a shallow sandy scoop lined with pastel houses and beached fishing skiffs. We usually drop the hook here for a long lunchtime swim, then take a berth on the town quay for the night. The western bay, Baia delle Favole, earned its fairy-tale name from Hans Christian Andersen, who wintered here. Late afternoon, walk the Punta Manara path out to the headland for a view back over both bays. Dinner is easy: trofie al pesto at any of the harbourside trattorie, the sauce made the Genoese way with a mortar.
thingsToDo: Swim the sandy scoop of Baia del Silenzio
Walk the Punta Manara headland trail
Order trofie al pesto at a harbour trattoria
Watch sunset over Baia delle Favole
1.mooringTip: The town quay takes you stern-to at roughly €50–80 a night and shelters well from the north. If it's full, anchor on sand in 4–6 m inside Baia del Silenzio and tender ashore.

2.shortDescription: Five easy miles east to Moneglia, a terracotta village behind a pebble shore, with a castle on the ridge and cheese focaccia at the port.
2.description: The leg to Moneglia barely counts as a passage, which is the point on a Riviera trip: short hops, long afternoons. The town tips down toward two pebble beaches, its roofline all terracotta and faded ochre. Anchor off Spiaggia di San Giorgio, where sand shelves gently under olive terraces and the holding is good in settled weather from the north. Above the village stands the Castello di Monleone, a squat sixteenth-century keep worth the climb for the coastline it commands. The water here is clear enough to spot octopus working the seagrass if you snorkel the edges of the bay. Ashore, the local vice is focaccia di Recco, that thin sheet of dough with molten stracchino between the layers, baked a few miles up the coast and best eaten warm at a portside osteria.
thingsToDo: Anchor off Spiaggia di San Giorgio
Climb to the Castello di Monleone
Eat focaccia di Recco at the port
Snorkel the seagrass for octopus
2.mooringTip: Moneglia has no lazy lines to speak of, so drop anchor on sand in 4–6 m off San Giorgio for the day. The handful of village quay slots (about €40–60) go early in July, so radio ahead if you want a berth.

3.shortDescription: Eight miles east to car-free Bonassola, gateway to a tunnel bike path and the boat-only coves of Framura just west.
3.description: Bonassola is a quiet one, a village of a thousand or so that keeps cars mostly at bay and lets you slow down. The great local secret is the old coastal railway: when the trains were rerouted inland, the tunnels became a flat, shaded cycle and walking path that runs east through the rock to Levanto and on toward the Cinque Terre. Hire a bike in the village and you can be looking down on the coast within twenty minutes. The small coves tucked under Framura, a mile or two west, are reachable only from the water and make a fine morning swim stop before you settle in. Anchor in the open bay off town on sand in 5–7 m. For dinner, look for salt-cured anchovies and a chilled Vermentino at a family-run osteria; the anchovy is a point of Ligurian pride.
thingsToDo: Cycle the old railway tunnel path
Swim the boat-only coves near Framura
Walk to the Madonnina della Punta chapel
Try salt-cured anchovies at an osteria
3.mooringTip: Anchoring in Bonassola's bay is free on good sand in 5–7 m, well protected from the north but open to any southerly swell. The village has only a few quay spaces, so treat it as an anchorage rather than a berth.

4.shortDescription: Six miles east into the Cinque Terre proper, landing at Monterosso, the biggest of the five and the only one with real sand.
4.description: Round Punta Mesco and the Cinque Terre open up, five villages stitched into terraced cliffs that have been farmed for wine since the Middle Ages. Monterosso is the westernmost and the largest, split between an old quarter and the Fegina seafront, where striped umbrellas run along the region's one genuine sand beach. There is no marina and the quay belongs to the ferries, so plan to anchor in the bay on sand in 6–8 m and go ashore by tender. Monterosso is the natural base for walking the Sentiero Azzurro, the blue trail that links all five villages along the cliffs. Save an evening for the local table: salted anchovies cured in the town's own way, a wedge of chickpea farinata, and a small glass of Sciacchetrà, the honeyed passito these steep vineyards are known for.
thingsToDo: Walk the Sentiero Azzurro toward Vernazza
Swim Fegina's sandy beach
Taste Monterosso anchovies and farinata
Sip a glass of Sciacchetrà
4.mooringTip: Anchor in Monterosso Bay on sand in 6–8 m, sheltered from the north; the quay is for ferries only, with no overnight berthing. In a southerly the bay gets rolly, so check the forecast before committing to the night.

5.shortDescription: A three-mile hop to Vernazza, the most photographed of the five, with a Doria tower standing guard over its tiny natural harbour.
5.description: Vernazza is the one everyone photographs, and for once the reputation holds up: a cluster of tall pastel houses wrapped around a pocket harbour, with the eleventh-century Castello Doria and its round watchtower on the rock above. The catch for sailors is the seabed. Holding in the bay is patchy over rock and the anchorage is exposed, so most crews treat Vernazza as a day stop and run the three miles back to Monterosso to sleep. Go in early, before the ferries and the trail walkers arrive, and you can have the harbour steps almost to yourself. Climb to the castle for the long view down the coast, then snorkel the clear water under the cliffs near Punta Mesco. Before you leave, a lemon granita on the harbour piazza is the right kind of cliché.
thingsToDo: Climb to the Castello Doria tower
Snorkel the cliffs near Punta Mesco
Photograph the harbour at first light
Cool off with a lemon granita
5.mooringTip: Expect moderate holding over rock in 6–8 m in Vernazza Bay, and no protection if the wind backs onshore. Anchor for the day only and take the overnight three miles back at Monterosso.

6.shortDescription: A fifteen-mile run west to the Portofino promontory, with a swim at turquoise Paraggi, the sea-only abbey of San Fruttuoso, and a Santa Margherita berth.
6.description: This is the day with real sea room, a fifteen-mile reach back west to the Portofino headland and the marine reserve that guards it. Paraggi is the prize: a small cove of pale sand and improbably green water just short of Portofino, part of the protected zone and picked up by mooring buoys in season. A little further west lies San Fruttuoso, a Benedictine abbey folded into a cove you can only reach by boat or by the coast path from Camogli. Snorkellers come for the Cristo degli Abissi, a bronze Christ set on the seabed at about fifteen metres. The reserve has zones and rules, so read the buoyage and don't anchor where you shouldn't. Overnight, drop back to the marina at Santa Margherita Ligure, where a plate of trenette al pesto rounds off the day.
thingsToDo: Swim the green water of Paraggi
Visit the sea-bound San Fruttuoso abbey
Snorkel over the Christ of the Abyss
Berth and dine at Santa Margherita
6.mooringTip: Inside the reserve you pick up a buoy at Paraggi for the day, and overnight anchoring in the protected zones is not allowed. Take a stern-to berth at Santa Margherita marina (roughly €100–160 in peak season) and book ahead in July and August.

7.shortDescription: A final eight miles home to Lavagna via Portofino, its pastel piazzetta and Castello Brown above a harbour that prices out charter yachts.
7.description: The last leg loops eight miles south back to Lavagna, and it would be a crime to pass Portofino without stopping. The village made its name as a fishing port and never quite stopped looking the part, a horseshoe of ochre houses around a piazzetta that now fills with superyacht crews. Castello Brown sits on the hill above, a short walk up through the olives for the classic view down onto the harbour mouth. Berthing inside is priced for a different kind of budget, north of several hundred euros a night for a charter boat, so anchor off the entrance on sand in 8–10 m and go in by tender for a coffee and a wander. Then it's a straight run back to Lavagna to refuel, rinse the salt off, and toast the week with an aperitivo before handover in the morning.
thingsToDo: Walk up to Castello Brown
Anchor off Portofino for a swim
Wander the Portofino piazzetta by tender
Aperitivo on the Lavagna waterfront
7.mooringTip: Anchor outside the Portofino entrance on sand in 8–10 m for a day visit rather than paying the harbour's premium. Back at Lavagna, take a lazy-line berth (about €80–120) with full fuel and services ready for the morning handover.

lipari.metaDesc: A 7-day yacht charter from Lipari around all seven Aeolian volcanic islands: Vulcano mud baths, Stromboli eruptions, Panarea, Salina and Filicudi.

1.shortDescription: Twenty miles south to the mainland for a gentle first leg, full provisioning, and Etna smoking on the horizon at dusk.
1.description: Most crews use this first leg to find their feet. It's a straightforward twenty miles down to Portorosa, one of the bigger charter marinas on the Sicilian mainland, and the passage usually runs on a steady Maestrale off the northwest at ten to fifteen knots. Nothing to catch you out. Portorosa has proper services: fuel, water, a chandlery, so it's the sensible place to load the boat for the week ahead. Provision here rather than counting on the islands, where a jar of capers can cost what a meal does. Ashore, grab pasta alla Norma at one of the quayside trattorias and eat late. If the air is clear at sundown, Etna sits smoking fifty miles to the south. A taxi up to the ruins at Tindari fills an afternoon if you arrive early.
thingsToDo: Pasta alla Norma on the quay
Provision the boat before the islands
Taxi to the Tindari archaeological site
Watch Etna smoke from the pier
1.mooringTip: Portorosa is stern-to on lazy lines, roughly €100-160 a night in peak. Well sheltered from the north, with a fuel berth at the entrance, so top up on the way in.

2.shortDescription: A short hop north to the sulphur island, where the crater hike and warmed shallows of the Pool of Venus fill an easy day.
2.description: Fifteen miles brings you onto the first proper Aeolian island, and you'll smell it before you see it. Vulcano still vents sulphur, and the standard overnight is Porto di Levante on the north side. The famous mud pool sits right at the foot of the harbour, though check locally whether it's open before you plan your afternoon around it. Better bet, in my book, is the walk up the Gran Cratere: about an hour of loose ash underfoot, and from the rim you look straight down into a steaming caldera with the whole archipelago laid out beyond. Snorkel the warm shallows near the Pool of Venus where thermal water bubbles up through the sand. Come evening, grilled swordfish at Trattoria Vulcano does the job. Moor on the far side from the vents and the smell fades.
thingsToDo: Hike the Gran Cratere rim (391 m)
Soak in the Laghetto di Fanghi mud
Snorkel the warm Pool of Venus vents
Grilled swordfish at Trattoria Vulcano
2.mooringTip: Porto di Levante takes you stern-to at €60-100 a night, sheltered from the north. Berth on the harbour's far side, away from the fumaroles, or the sulphur will be in your cabin all night.

3.shortDescription: Twenty miles west to the wildest island in the chain, all donkey tracks and a lone rock spire rising sheer from the sea.
3.description: This is the one that feels furthest from anywhere. Filicudi has maybe two hundred and fifty people, no real road to speak of, and donkeys still carrying loads up the village paths. Off the west coast stands La Canna, an eighty-five-metre volcanic needle punching straight out of deep water, and the snorkelling around its base is the best on the route. Take the tender into the Grotta del Bue Marino while the light's low and the cave glows. Ashore, the Bronze Age village at Capo Graziano is a short scramble worth the sweat for the view alone. Tie up at little Filicudi Porto on the east coast, or drop the hook off Pecorini a Mare and dinghy in for pasta con le sarde at a quayside taverna. Leave by nine if the forecast is building.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the base of La Canna spire
Tender into the Grotta del Bue Marino
Scramble to the Capo Graziano ruins
Pasta con le sarde at Pecorini
3.mooringTip: Filicudi Porto is small and fills fast, stern-to at €60-90 a night with shelter from the north. If it's full, anchor off Pecorini a Mare on good sand in five to seven metres.

4.shortDescription: The long leg of the week, ending at the only island where you drop anchor at dusk to watch lava roll into the sea.
4.description: Thirty-two miles, the longest passage of the week, so we usually slip the lines early and make Stromboli by mid-afternoon. It's worth the miles. The cone throws up an eruption every fifteen or twenty minutes, day and night, and on the northwest flank the Sciara del Fuoco carries the glowing rock all the way down to the water. The trick is timing: potter through the afternoon at the Scari quay on the south side, then motor round and anchor off the Sciara as the light goes. From the deck you watch the whole thing in the dark, no crowds, drink in hand. Earlier, walk up to the four-hundred-metre viewpoint, which needs no guide, and stop at L'Osservatorio for a cannolo with the volcano filling the window. The white cubes of car-free Ginostra reward a nose around too.
thingsToDo: Anchor off the Sciara del Fuoco at dusk
Cannolo at L'Osservatorio, volcano in view
Walk up to the 400 m viewpoint
Wander car-free Ginostra's white lanes
4.mooringTip: You cannot lie overnight off the Sciara del Fuoco, so anchor there only for the show, on rock in eight to twelve metres, then move. For sleep, the Scari quay is stern-to at €40-60 in settled weather.

5.shortDescription: A dozen miles to the smallest and glossiest island in the group, where the anchorages come with underwater bubbles and a cliff-cut bar.
5.description: Panarea is tiny and knows its own worth. This is where Milan comes to sun itself in August, so book the marina ahead and expect the prices to match the postcards. Off the south coast, Cala Junco is a near-perfect horseshoe under the Bronze Age huts of Punta Milazzese, and it shelves gently onto sand at four to six metres for a lazy swim stop. A mile east, drop over the side at Lisca Bianca and you'll swim through columns of fumarole gas rising off the seabed like champagne. Ashore at San Pietro, the studios turn out hand-painted ceramics worth carrying home, and when the sun's down the Raya bar cranks up on its cliff terrace. It's a short, gentle sail, so linger over the snorkelling and arrive with the afternoon still ahead of you.
thingsToDo: Swim into Cala Junco below the ruins
Snorkel the champagne bubbles at Lisca Bianca
Sundowner on the Raya bar terrace
Hand-painted ceramics from San Pietro studios
5.mooringTip: Marina San Pietro is stern-to and pricey in peak, €100-160 a night, sheltered from the north. Book well ahead in August, or day-anchor in Cala Junco on sand at four to six metres and skip the crowds.

6.shortDescription: Eight green miles to the garden of the archipelago, where the wine runs sweet, the granita is famous, and Pollara's cliffs star in a film.
6.description: After a week of ash and bare rock, Salina comes as a green surprise. Two old volcanic cones rise over vineyards and caper terraces, and this is the only island in the chain with water enough to farm properly. Tie up at Santa Marina on the east coast. The local Malvasia is a sweet amber wine you should taste at source, and Hauner is the name to look for. Down in the hamlet of Lingua, Da Alfredo has been serving granita di gelsi, made with mulberries, to a queue of regulars for decades; order it with a warm brioche and don't rush. Fit crews can grind up Monte Fossa delle Felci, nearly a thousand metres, for a four-hour round trip. On the far side, the cliffside village of Pollara stood in for the setting of Il Postino back in the nineties.
thingsToDo: Taste Malvasia delle Lipari at Hauner
Granita di gelsi at Da Alfredo in Lingua
Walk the Pollara cliffs from Il Postino
Climb Monte Fossa delle Felci (962 m)
6.mooringTip: Santa Marina Salina is stern-to at €60-100 a night with good northerly shelter. If the marina is full, Rinella on the south coast is a quieter fallback with room to anchor.

7.shortDescription: A short six miles home to the capital, with a last swim under the white pumice cliffs before the boat goes back.
7.description: The last leg is barely an hour, so there's no need to rush breakfast. On the way back to Lipari, swing by the old pumice quarries on the north coast off Canneto and Acquacalda, where the spoil from a century of mining has turned the cliffs chalk-white and the shallows an improbable turquoise. Slide in for a final swim before the crowds. Lipari town itself is the busy hub of the islands, and Marina Lunga is where you'll hand the boat back, so refuel at the fuel berth on the way in and confirm your slot the day before. With time ashore, the Archaeological Museum up in the castle is genuinely good, the Norman cathedral sits at the top of the old lanes, and a limoncello down on Marina Corta is a fair way to close the week.
thingsToDo: Swim the white pumice quarries off Canneto
Aeolian Archaeological Museum in the castle
Walk to the Norman cathedral lanes
Limoncello on Marina Corta
7.mooringTip: Marina Lunga is stern-to at €70-120 a night in peak and has the island's fuel berth at the entrance, so top up before you tie up. Confirm the handover slot 24 hours ahead.

olbia-long.metaDesc: Round-trip yacht charter from Olbia through Tavolara, the Maddalena islands and Bonifacio in Corsica, then home via Costa Smeralda over seven days.

1.shortDescription: A gentle 15-mile hop south to shake down the boat under Tavolara's 565-metre limestone wall, then quiet nights across the water at Porto San Paolo.
1.description: Most crews spend the first afternoon getting acquainted with the boat, and this leg is built for exactly that. Fifteen miles down the coast, Tavolara climbs almost vertically out of the water, a slab of grey limestone that once styled itself the smallest kingdom on earth. We usually drop the hook at Spalmatore di Terra, sand at four to six metres, well tucked from anything out of the north. The swim here sets the tone for the week: pale bottom, sharp shade under the cliff. Ashore, a rough track leads to the little cemetery where the island's self-declared royals are buried, worth the twenty minutes it takes. This is a day anchorage only, so once the light softens we motor back across to Porto San Paolo and tie up opposite for a settled first night.
thingsToDo: Swim the sand at Spalmatore di Terra
Walk to the island kingdom's cemetery
Free-dive Tavolara's underwater limestone arches
Lunch ashore at Da Tonino taverna
1.mooringTip: Spalmatore di Terra is a daytime anchorage only, sand at 4-6 m and sheltered from the north. Plan to overnight in the small marina at Porto San Paolo across the channel, around EUR 60-90 a night.

2.shortDescription: Twenty miles north into the Maddalena park to Cala Coticcio, the boat-only cove they nickname the Med's Tahiti, with Garibaldi's old island next door.
2.description: The run north crosses into the Maddalena archipelago, a granite maze that rewards an early start. Caprera is where Garibaldi settled in 1855 and stayed until his death, and his low white house still stands with the furniture more or less as he left it. The draw for most crews is Cala Coticcio on the eastern side, reachable only from the water, its granite arms wrapped around a sand bottom that glows turquoise by midday. In July and August the park requires you to book a mooring buoy online before you arrive, and the popular buoys go fast. If they are gone, Cala Portese on the south side gives you a free anchorage over sand at five to seven metres, less dramatic but perfectly calm in settled weather.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim at Cala Coticcio
Tour Garibaldi's house and tomb
Walk the trail to Punta Rossa fort
Snorkel over sand at Cala Portese
2.mooringTip: Cala Coticcio's park buoys run about EUR 40-80 a night at peak and must be reserved online through the Maddalena park. If they're taken, drop anchor free over sand at 5-7 m in Cala Portese.

3.shortDescription: A five-mile skip west to uninhabited Spargi, where pink-granite boulders frame Cala Corsara and tidal rock pools sit just around the southern point.
3.description: Barely an hour separates Caprera from Spargi, so there's no rush to weigh anchor. The island has no residents and no road, just pink-tinged granite piled into shapes the wind has been working on for a very long time. Cala Corsara on the southwest corner is the anchorage everyone comes for, sand at four to six metres and good holding once the north wind eases. Take the tender or swim around to the Piscine Naturali on the south shore, a set of shallow pools warm enough to linger in. Afternoons here tend to slow right down. We often leave the deck rigged for shade, pull out lunch, and let the crew drift between the boat and the beach until the light turns gold and the day-trip boats clear out.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim at Cala Corsara
Snorkel the Piscine Naturali rock pools
Beachcomb the empty southern shoreline
Shade lunch on deck under the granite
3.mooringTip: Book a park buoy at Cala Corsara online, roughly EUR 40-60 a night in high season. When none are free you can anchor alongside on sand at 4-6 m, though the cove empties of wind more reliably than of day boats.

4.shortDescription: Twelve miles north over the notorious Bonifacio Strait into Corsica, tying up beneath the limestone cliffs of Bonifacio's cliff-cut fjord harbour.
4.description: Today's the passage to respect. The Bonifacio Strait squeezes the Mistral between Sardinia and Corsica and can add five to eight knots to whatever's forecast, which makes it the windiest stretch of the western Med. We cross only in daylight and only when the outlook stays under twenty-five knots; otherwise the plan is simply to wait. Get the timing right and it's twelve straightforward miles to the Corsican shore, where Bonifacio hides its harbour inside a slot of white limestone, the old town balanced on a seventy-metre cliff directly above the water. You berth stern-to deep in the inner fjord, fully protected once you're in. Climb up to the citadel that evening. The walls, the lanes, and the light off the chalk rock all reward the effort of getting there.
thingsToDo: Walk the citadel ramparts at dusk
Descend the King of Aragon's Steps
Tender into the limestone sea caves
Langoustine risotto ashore at U Castille
4.mooringTip: Berth stern-to inside the inner fjord at Marina de Bonifacio, around EUR 100-180 a night at peak and completely sheltered. Only attempt the strait crossing in daylight with the Mistral forecast under 25 knots.

5.shortDescription: Back across the strait, eight miles south to Santa Teresa Gallura, Sardinia's northernmost port and one of the few coves sheltered from every wind.
5.description: Pick a calm window and the return leg is short, eight miles back over the strait to the Sardinian side. Santa Teresa Gallura sits behind a headland that shrugs off wind from any quarter, which makes it a reassuring place to tie up after a lively couple of days. The Longonsardo tower, thrown up by the Aragonese in the sixteenth century, keeps watch over the entrance. Anchor off Rena Bianca, a short white crescent right below the town, for the afternoon swim. If there's time, take the road out to Capo Testa, where the granite has been carved by weather into hollows and mushrooms that photographers spend hours on. Evenings belong to the Piazza Vittorio Emanuele, where a glass of mirto after dinner is close to compulsory.
thingsToDo: Swim off the Rena Bianca crescent
Climb the 16th-century Longonsardo tower
Wander the wind-carved rocks at Capo Testa
Sip mirto on Piazza Vittorio Emanuele
5.mooringTip: Berth stern-to in Santa Teresa Gallura's marina, roughly EUR 60-100 a night and sheltered from every direction. Rena Bianca just outside works as a fair-weather anchor stop on sand at 4-6 m.

6.shortDescription: An easy twelve miles east to Palau, the working gateway to the Maddalena islands, with the wind-carved Bear Rock a short drive along the coast.
6.description: Palau is a proper working port, the jumping-off point for the Maddalena islands and only a short ferry ride from La Maddalena town itself. The sail across is undemanding, twelve miles of familiar water, so there's room in the day for something ashore. The obvious outing is Capo d'Orso, a granite headland a few miles east where erosion has shaped one boulder into an unmistakable crouching bear; the walk up from the road is steep but brief and the view over the archipelago earns it. Back in the harbour, look for malloreddus, the little saffron-tinted Sardinian gnocchi, at one of the port trattorias. Come evening, wander round to Porto Rafael, a low-key cluster of pastel houses where the light on the water at sunset is about as good as this coast gets.
thingsToDo: Hike up to the Capo d'Orso Bear Rock
Ferry across to La Maddalena town
Eat malloreddus at a port trattoria
Watch the sunset from Porto Rafael
6.mooringTip: Take a stern-to berth at Marina di Palau, about EUR 80-120 a night at peak and sheltered from the north. Marina dell'Orso a mile east is the bigger fallback if Palau is full.

7.shortDescription: Twenty miles south down the Costa Smeralda, a last swim off Porto Cervo before refuelling and handing the boat back at Olbia.
7.description: The last leg runs twenty miles south down the Costa Smeralda, and there's usually time to fit in one more swim before the boat goes back. Cala Granu, tucked just outside Porto Cervo and reachable only from the water, is a fine spot to drop the hook over sand at five to seven metres while the crew has a final float. If anyone fancies a look at how the other half moors, the marble Piazzetta up in Porto Cervo is a short walk from the quay. From there it's a straight run into Olbia. Top up at the fuel berth by the entrance, give the boat a proper clean, and confirm the handover slot the yard will have asked you to hold. A last dinner at a Porto San Paolo trattoria closes the week where it more or less began.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim at Cala Granu
Stroll the marble Porto Cervo Piazzetta
Refuel and wash down at Olbia Marina
Farewell dinner at a Porto San Paolo trattoria
7.mooringTip: Berth stern-to with lazy lines at Olbia Marina, roughly EUR 100-150 a night at peak. Refuel at the fuel berth by the entrance and confirm your handover slot with the base at least 24 hours ahead.

olbia.metaTitle: 7-Day Costa Smeralda Loop from Olbia | Boat4You
olbia.metaDesc: Sail 7 days from Olbia through Tavolara, Golfo Aranci, Porto Rotondo, Portisco, Porto Cervo and the Maddalena park. Anchorages, moorings and honest tips.

1.shortDescription: A gentle twelve-mile run south to shake down the boat, ending at a fishing village that sits square opposite the Tavolara wall.
1.description: Most crews use the first leg to find the boat rather than push it, and twelve miles down from Olbia gives you exactly that. The wind here is usually the Mistral off the northwest, ten to twenty-five knots that funnels harder as it squeezes through the Bonifacio Strait; the good news is that these southern coves sit in its lee. Porto San Paolo itself is a working fishing village, low-key and unpolished, staring straight across at the limestone hulk of Tavolara. We tend to drop the hook in Cala Girgolu, four to six metres over clean sand, and swim before dinner. Ashore, the local bottarga is worth seeking out. Grilled octopus off a quayside grill and a cold Vermentino makes a fair reward for a travel day.
thingsToDo: Anchor and snorkel the Cala Girgolu seagrass
Track down real Sardinian bottarga ashore
Walk up to Capo Coda Cavallo
Grilled octopus at a quayside trattoria
1.mooringTip: Porto San Paolo's small marina takes you stern-to for roughly €60-90 a night and shelters you from the north. If you'd rather swing on the hook, Cala Girgolu holds well on sand in four to six metres.

2.shortDescription: A four-mile nip across to the island that once crowned its own king, with a sand beach tucked under a sheer limestone face.
2.description: Tavolara is a genuine oddity. The rock climbs more than five hundred metres straight out of the water, and for a stretch of the nineteenth century a shepherd family here ran what history remembers as the world's smallest kingdom. Their little cemetery still sits ashore, headstones and all. The eastern end is a military zone, so keep your visit to the western spit. We anchor off Spalmatore di Terra, sand at four to six metres, protected from the north, and treat it as a lunch stop rather than a bed for the night. The beach is pale and shallow; the free-diving under the limestone overhangs is the real draw. Da Tonino, the taverna that has fed sailors here for decades, is worth booking ahead if you want a table.
thingsToDo: Swim off Spiaggia Spalmatore's pale sand
Find the old royal cemetery ashore
Free-dive the limestone overhangs
Book a table at Da Tonino
2.mooringTip: There's no marina here, so it's anchor-only on sand at four to six metres off Spalmatore di Terra. Keep it a daytime stop and move on before nightfall if the Mistral is forecast above about eighteen knots.

3.shortDescription: Twelve miles north to a low-key ferry town with a fully sheltered berth and a submerged mountain a mile offshore worth diving.
3.description: Golfo Aranci earns its place on the itinerary less for the town than for what sits under the water just outside it. This is the northeast corner of Sardinia, a modest port where mainland ferries come and go, and the marina gives you a proper night's sleep behind full shelter. A mile east lies the Secca di Capo Figari, an underwater peak that rises toward the surface and pulls in grouper, barracuda and, if you're lucky with the light, plenty of colour. Bring a mask. Onshore, the Capo Figari headland has a walking track up to an old lighthouse with the whole gulf laid out below. Provisioning is easy here, which makes it a sensible stop before the prices climb further up the Costa Smeralda.
thingsToDo: Dive the Secca di Capo Figari
Hike to the Capo Figari lighthouse
Swim off the white sand at Spiaggia Bianca
Restock before Costa Smeralda prices climb
3.mooringTip: Marina di Golfo Aranci puts you stern-to on lazy lines, around €100-150 in high season, and it's fully protected. In August book ahead; the berths fill early.

4.shortDescription: A short six-mile hop into a purpose-built resort of marble squares and bougainvillea, where the marina bills match the postcard.
4.description: Porto Rotondo is a designed place, laid out from scratch in the early 1960s by a Venetian family with an eye for theatre. The result is all pale stone piazzas, stepped amphitheatre seating and villas half-buried in flowers. It photographs beautifully and charges accordingly; a marina berth in August runs among the steepest on the whole Mediterranean coast. If the numbers make you wince, anchor outside in the bay on sand at six to eight metres and tender in for a look. The pink-granite cove at Spiaggia Ira, just around the point, is a fine morning swim before the day-boats arrive. Come evening the crowd dresses up for aperitivo on the marble; you don't have to join in, but it's a show worth watching once.
thingsToDo: Morning swim at Spiaggia Ira's pink granite
Aperitivo on the Piazzetta San Marco marble
Visit the modernist San Lorenzo church
Sit in the open-air amphitheatre at dusk
4.mooringTip: Marina di Porto Rotondo is premium and priced like it, roughly €200-400 a night at peak. To dodge that, anchor out in the bay on sand at six to eight metres and use the tender; there's no shore power out there, so plan your batteries.

5.shortDescription: A four-mile shuffle to the region's main charter base, a working marina within a mile of one of Sardinia's showiest bays.
5.description: Portisco is where a lot of these charters actually begin and end, and it shows in the setup: a big, well-run quay with lazy lines, fuel, water and someone who answers the radio. It isn't pretty in the resort sense, but it's practical, and that has its own value mid-week. The reason to stop is next door. Cala di Volpe, a mile east, is the bay the Aga Khan's set made famous in the sixties, and the water still lives up to the reputation. For something quieter, Cupaccia is reachable only by boat and tends to stay calm. Eat well here: culurgiones, the mint-and-potato parcels pinched shut by hand, are a Sardinian staple worth ordering. If you fancy a late one, the cliff-top beach clubs nearby run their sunsets hard.
thingsToDo: Swim in the celebrated Cala di Volpe
Tender to boat-only Cupaccia for quiet
Order handmade culurgiones at a local trattoria
Catch a cliff-top sunset at a beach club
5.mooringTip: Marina di Portisco takes you stern-to on lazy lines with full services, around €120-200 at peak. It's the busiest base on this coast, so radio ahead in August and expect to queue for fuel late afternoon.

6.shortDescription: Six miles up to the Costa's showpiece harbour, worth a swim in a boat-only cove before you commit to the eye-watering berth.
6.description: Porto Cervo is the reason a lot of people point a bow at this coast in the first place. Founded by the Aga Khan in 1962 as the flagship of the whole Costa Smeralda project, it has spent sixty years being the most expensive marina in the Mediterranean, and August rates hold that title without much competition. My advice is to earn the visit before you pay for it. Cala Granu, just short of the harbour, is boat-only and holds nicely on sand in five to seven metres; swim there first. Then decide whether the berth is worth it or whether you'd rather anchor and tender in for the evening. The old Piazzetta, with its curved marble and stepped alleys, is genuinely good architecture. The boutiques speak for themselves.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim in boat-only Cala Granu
Walk the curved marble of the Piazzetta
Watch the superyachts in the Old Port
Window-shop the boutique alleys
6.mooringTip: Marina di Porto Cervo is the priciest slot on this route, roughly €300-600 a night in August, full premium service. For a fraction of that, anchor in Cala Granu on sand at five to seven metres; there's no shore power, so top up your batteries first.

7.shortDescription: The long twenty-mile leg home, timed to detour through the Maddalena park for its most famous cove before refuelling back at Olbia.
7.description: The final day is the longest, so we usually slip the lines early and take the scenic way south through the Maddalena archipelago. Caprera is the target. Cala Coticcio, on its eastern side, carries the nickname "Tahiti of the Med," and for once the marketing undersells it: pale sand, water in improbable greens, and a headland that keeps the northerly out. Access is tightly controlled now, so sort your park permit before you arrive. Caprera also gave Garibaldi his final home, and his house is open as a museum if the timing works. From there it's a steady run back down to Olbia. Leave enough in the tank to top up at the fuel berth, give the boat a rinse, and you'll hand her back clean.
thingsToDo: Anchor at Cala Coticcio, the 'Tahiti of the Med'
Swim at Cala Corsara on Spargi
Tour Garibaldi's house museum on Caprera
Refuel at Olbia's fuel berth before handover
7.mooringTip: Back at Olbia Marina it's stern-to on lazy lines, about €100-150 at peak, with a fuel berth by the entrance for the pre-handover top-up. One catch: Cala Coticcio now needs a park permit booked online for July and August, and numbers are capped, so arrange it well ahead.

palermo.metaTitle: 7-Day Egadi Islands Loop from Palermo | Boat4You
palermo.metaDesc: Sail a 7-day round trip from Palermo through the Egadi Islands: San Vito Lo Capo, Favignana, Marettimo and Levanzo, with mooring and anchorage notes.

1.shortDescription: A 32-mile shakedown west past the Zingaro cliffs to a rare Sicilian sand beach, a good first day to test the rig.
1.description: The first leg out of Palermo is long enough to shake the crew into shape without punishing anyone. Thirty-two miles west, mostly on a beam reach if the usual northwesterly fills in by late morning. The Zingaro reserve slides past to starboard, seven kilometres of undeveloped limestone with no legal anchorage inside its boundary, so admire it and keep going. San Vito Lo Capo waits at the end, its harbour tucked under Monte Monaco. The town beach is genuinely white sand, unusual on this stretch where most swimming is off rock. We usually tie up by mid-afternoon, walk the seafront, and find couscous alla trapanese at one of the quayside trattorias. San Vito takes its couscous seriously; the September festival is proof enough.
thingsToDo: Swim the white-sand crescent at San Vito
Walk the Zingaro reserve coastal path
Couscous alla trapanese on the quay
Sunset from behind Monte Monaco
1.mooringTip: San Vito's little harbour takes you stern-to for roughly €60–100 a night and shelters well from the north. If it's full, drop the hook in the bay just outside over sand in 5–7 metres.

2.shortDescription: Twenty miles southwest to the butterfly-shaped biggest Egadi, where the red rock of Cala Rossa marks an ancient Roman sea battle.
2.description: Favignana is shaped like a butterfly, split by the ridge of Monte Santa Caterina with its old fort on top. The crossing from San Vito runs about twenty miles, usually an easy downwind slide in summer. Cala Rossa on the east side is the anchorage everyone talks about, and for once the hype holds: pale limestone quarried into strange shelves, water that glows over a sand bottom. The Romans and Carthaginians fought their decisive battle in these waters in 241 BC. Ashore, the old Florio tonnara still dominates the port, a cathedral of a tuna factory now gone silent. Rent a bike if you have an afternoon; the island is flat and small. For dinner, busiate twisted around pesto trapanese is the plate to order.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim at Cala Rossa
Wander the silent Florio tonnara
Cycle to Bue Marino quarry cove
Busiate with pesto trapanese for dinner
2.mooringTip: Favignana port takes you stern-to at around €60–100 a night with good shelter from the north. For a lunch stop instead, anchor off Cala Rossa on sand in 4–6 metres and watch for the afternoon breeze curling in.

3.shortDescription: Fifteen miles out to the wildest Egadi: no cars, donkeys hauling the freight, and a Norman castle watching the northern headland.
3.description: Marettimo sits furthest out, exposed to whatever the open Mediterranean sends, so pick a settled forecast for this one. Fifteen miles west of Favignana, and the character changes completely. Around seven hundred people live here, no road network beyond the village, goods still moving by donkey. The harbour is small and the swimming is off cliffs rather than beaches. Cala Bianca on the south coast is the classic stop, white limestone dropping into deep blue. If the crew has legs, the path north climbs to the twelfth-century Punta Troia castle on its rock. Divers come for the grottoes; the Grotta del Cammello is the one to snorkel. Da Enzo in the village does pesce alla stemperata, sweet and sour with capers and olives, when the catch allows.
thingsToDo: Swim the limestone cliffs at Cala Bianca
Hike donkey trails to Punta Troia castle
Snorkel the Grotta del Cammello
Pesce alla stemperata in the village
3.mooringTip: Marettimo's harbour is tight, stern-to at roughly €40–60 a night and sheltered from the north, so aim to arrive early for a slot. Cala Bianca works as a daytime anchorage over sand in 5–7 metres in calm weather only.

4.shortDescription: A short twelve-mile hop east to the smallest Egadi, home to a cave where Stone Age hands painted the walls.
4.description: Levanzo is the smallest of the inhabited Egadi and the quietest, a single whitewashed village around a scoop of a harbour and maybe two hundred residents. The run from Marettimo is only twelve miles, an easy morning. The reason to come is the Grotta del Genovese on the north coast, where Neolithic painters and, further back, Ice Age hands left images of deer and tuna on the rock. You reach it by guided tour booked in the village, either on foot or by boat. Cala Fredda on the east side is the swimming stop, pebbles underfoot and clear water lapping in. In the evening, order ricci, the local sea urchins, with a glass of Nero d'Avola and watch the harbour go still.
thingsToDo: Book the Grotta del Genovese cave-art tour
Swim off the pebbles at Cala Fredda
Sea urchins with a glass of Nero d'Avola
Walk to Cala Tramontana on the north shore
4.mooringTip: Levanzo's quay has only a handful of stern-to berths at €30–50 a night, so it pays to arrive early or plan to anchor. Cala Fredda holds well on sand in 4–6 metres if the port is taken.

5.shortDescription: Twenty miles back northeast toward the mainland, with a swim below the Zingaro cliffs and a sanctuary sunset waiting ashore.
5.description: Turning for the mainland, the leg back to San Vito Lo Capo runs about twenty miles northeast across open water. In settled weather it's worth breaking the passage with a swim at Cala Mancina, on the seaward side of the Zingaro reserve; you cannot anchor inside the protected zone, so you swim from just outside it. Back at San Vito, the harbour is familiar from the first night, which makes the approach easy at the end of a long day. Ashore, the climb to the Santuario di Santa Crocifissa di Custonaci rewards you with the coast lit up at dusk. Cool off afterwards with almond granita in town. It's a slower day than the outbound legs, and the crew usually welcomes it.
thingsToDo: Swim at Cala Mancina outside the reserve
Climb to the Santa Crocifissa sanctuary
Almond granita back in San Vito
Second walk along the sand beach
5.mooringTip: You're back to San Vito's harbour, stern-to at €60–100 a night and well sheltered from the north. Same berth as night one, so you already know the approach and the holding.

6.shortDescription: Fifteen miles east along the coast to Terrasini, a working fishing town of Baroque squares just short of Palermo's bay.
6.description: Terrasini is a working fishing harbour rather than a resort, which is part of its appeal after the islands. The hop east from San Vito is about fifteen miles, hugging the coast most of the way. The old centre gathers around a Baroque square, and the fish market near the port is the real draw at the right hour. Anchor off Cala Rossa di Terrasini for a swim, or take a berth on the town quay. A mile west, the Capo Rama reserve has sea caves worth a snorkel if the sea stays calm. This is citrus country, so buy blood oranges while you can. For dinner, pasta con le sarde, with sardines, wild fennel and a scatter of pine nuts, is the dish that says you have reached the Palermo side.
thingsToDo: Swim off Cala Rossa di Terrasini
Snorkel the Capo Rama sea caves
Buy blood oranges at the fish market
Pasta con le sarde for dinner
6.mooringTip: Terrasini's town quay takes you stern-to at around €40–60 a night with shelter from the north. If it's busy, Cala Rossa di Terrasini anchors well on sand in 4–6 metres.

7.shortDescription: A final fifteen miles into Palermo's bay, with an optional swim at Capo Gallo before the noise and mosaics of the city.
7.description: The last leg is short, fifteen miles back east into Palermo's bay. With time in hand, drop anchor for a final swim at the Capo Gallo reserve or off Isola delle Femmine before the marina swallows the trip. Top up fuel before you tie up; it saves a scramble on handover morning. La Cala sits right in the old harbour, central and loud, while Villa Igiea offers calmer, pricier berths across the bay. Then Palermo itself: the Vucciria market at dusk, all shouting vendors and grilled offal, and the Palatine Chapel with its ceiling of Norman-Arab mosaics. Grab a panino con panelle, the chickpea fritter roll, from a street stall. It's the right, chaotic full stop after a week of quiet anchorages.
thingsToDo: Final swim at the Capo Gallo reserve
Vucciria market at dusk
Palatine Chapel Norman-Arab mosaics
Panino con panelle from a street stall
7.mooringTip: La Cala inside the old port runs €100–160 a night in peak season; Villa Igiea across the bay is the pricier, more polished alternative. Refuel at the entrance fuel berth before taking your slot.

palermoLong.metaDesc: Round-trip from Palermo: seven days to Mondello, Cefalù and the Aeolian volcanoes of Lipari, Salina and Vulcano, then home along the Sicilian coast.

1.shortDescription: An easy eight-mile warm-up north to Mondello, the sandy bay under Monte Pellegrino where Palermitani swim on summer weekends.
1.description: Most crews use the first afternoon to shake the boat down rather than cover ground, and eight miles suits that fine. We usually slip out of Marina Cala after the handover, round the headland below Monte Pellegrino, and have the anchor down off Mondello inside a couple of hours. The bay is a shallow scoop of sand, pale green water over four to six metres, well tucked from any northerly. Ashore, the old bathing pier sits on stilts above the shallows, a leftover from the town's early-1900s heyday. Swim, sort the fridge, learn where everything lives on board. If you want a proper first dinner, the fritto misto and pasta con le sarde in the village hold up. Those keen on a walk can climb the switchbacks up Pellegrino for the view back over the Conca d'Oro.
thingsToDo: Drop the hook and swim off Mondello
Stroll the stilted Liberty-era bathing pier
Climb Monte Pellegrino for the Conca d'Oro view
Try pasta con le sarde in the village
1.mooringTip: The little marina at Mondello takes yachts stern-to for roughly €60–90 a night and shelters you from the north; otherwise drop the hook on sand in four to six metres and run back to Marina Cala for a secure overnight.

2.shortDescription: A 32-mile reach east to Cefalù, the Norman cathedral town crouched beneath La Rocca, with a real marina for the night.
2.description: Worth leaving by nine to make the miles comfortable before the afternoon sea breeze gets up. The coast east of Palermo runs low and built-up for a while, then the ground rises and Cefalù announces itself: a wall of medieval stone under the great crag of La Rocca. The marina takes you stern-to and sits well sheltered from the north, a short walk from the old town. The twin-towered cathedral is the reason to come ashore, its 1130s apse mosaics among the finest Byzantine work in Sicily. Later, climb the stepped path up La Rocca to the ruined castle and the so-called Temple of Diana; the light softens after five and the view down onto the tiled roofs and the beach earns the sweat. Eat where the lanes meet the water and order the local anchovies.
thingsToDo: See the 12th-century cathedral's Byzantine mosaics
Climb La Rocca to the Temple of Diana
Swim the marble pebbles at Mazzaforno
Sunset drinks on the Bastione waterfront
2.mooringTip: Marina di Cefalù puts you stern-to for around €80–120 at peak and keeps you out of the northerly swell; book ahead in July and August. In settled weather you can anchor off the bay in five to seven metres of sand instead.

3.shortDescription: Thirty-eight miles of open water northeast lands you at Lipari, the Aeolian capital, its white pumice bluffs dropping into clear blue shallows.
3.description: This is the day that commits you to the islands, so treat it as a passage: full fuel and water, an early start, watches set. Thirty-eight miles of open Tyrrhenian usually means motoring through a glassy morning and sailing the back half once the breeze fills. Stromboli's plume shows itself long before Lipari does. Come in to Marina Lunga at the town and top up at the fuel berth by the entrance before you take a berth. Once you're settled, the standout swim is up the north coast at the old pumice quarries, where white spoil slopes run straight into pale turquoise shallows. Back in town, the archaeological museum in the castle is genuinely first-rate on Aeolian prehistory. Finish with a plate of fried squid and a glass of Malvasia down on little Marina Corta as the ferries come and go.
thingsToDo: Swim the white pumice quarries on the north coast
Tour the Aeolian archaeology museum in the castle
Wander the cathedral lanes above Marina Corta
Fried totani and Malvasia at Marina Corta
3.mooringTip: Marina Lunga berths you stern-to at roughly €70–120 in peak season; call the fuel berth at the harbour mouth and refuel on the way in, since space tightens fast on August evenings.

4.shortDescription: A lazy six miles across to Salina, the green one, twin peaks, mulberry granita on the quay and the Pollara cliffs from Il Postino.
4.description: After the long haul, a gentle one. Salina lies six miles off, close enough that there's no rush to leave; we tend to swim first and cross late morning. It's the wettest and greenest of the group, two old volcanoes side by side, and the only island here with springs enough for real farming. Tie up at Santa Marina on the east side, well protected from the north. The set-piece walk is Monte Fossa delle Felci, 962 metres and the highest point in the Aeolians, four hours up through fern and chestnut to a rim view over the whole archipelago. If that's too much in the heat, taxi over to Pollara, the drowned crater where they filmed Il Postino, and swim beneath the cliffs. Whatever you do, join the queue at Da Alfredo on the front for a mulberry granita. The capers and Malvasia here are the real thing.
thingsToDo: Mulberry granita at Da Alfredo on the quay
Hike Monte Fossa delle Felci, 962 metres
Taste Malvasia and capers at Hauner
Swim the Pollara crater from Il Postino
4.mooringTip: Santa Marina Salina takes you stern-to for about €60–100 a night and is snug in northerlies; if the harbour's full or you want quiet, Rinella on the south coast is the fallback.

5.shortDescription: Twelve miles south in the lee of Lipari to Vulcano, where the sulphur greets you before the harbour and the crater rim tops it all.
5.description: Twelve miles down the chain, mostly sheltered behind Lipari, so an easy sail if the afternoon westerly obliges. Hold close along Lipari's east side past the pale pumice above Canneto, then come through the Bocche di Vulcano into Porto di Levante. You'll smell the island before you tie up. Get ashore early for the Gran Cratere: the path starts just south of the port and climbs 391 metres in under an hour to a rim that looks straight down into the crater and out across every island in the group. It's the best short walk in the Aeolians, but the summit closes when gas readings spike, so ask before you set off and carry water. Down at sea level the fumarole-warmed shallows by the beach stay bath-warm, and the mud pools by the harbour open some seasons and not others. Cross the neck of the island on foot for sunset at the black sand of Sabbie Nere.
thingsToDo: Climb the Gran Cratere rim at first light
Soak the fumarole-warmed shallows by the beach
Ask if the harbour mud pools are open
Walk to Sabbie Nere black sand for sunset
5.mooringTip: Levante runs on local buoy moorings, so phone the concession ahead in high summer and reckon on a steady stream of ferry and hydrofoil wash. Prefer the hook? The black sand at Porto di Ponente grips nicely in three to eight metres, though it sits wide open to any northwesterly.

6.shortDescription: The big return leg: 46 open miles back to Sicily, so be off the mooring by dawn and aim to reach Cefalù by evening.
6.description: The longest single hop of the week, and it pays to be off the mooring by six with coffee made in the dark. Clear the Bocche di Vulcano and steer roughly 240 degrees for the Sicilian coast. Mornings out here usually start dead calm and you'll motor, but the northwesterly often fills after lunch and hands you the last hours under sail. It's the loneliest water of the trip; keep an eye astern for the hydrofoils funnelling into Milazzo and run proper watches so nobody's fried by arrival. La Rocca, the 268-metre pillar behind Cefalù, lifts over the horizon a long way out and guides you in. Berth at Porto Presidiana on the east flank of the headland, then walk ten minutes into the old town. Save enough energy for the climb up La Rocca in the last of the light, and a long dinner in the lanes off Via Vittorio Emanuele afterwards.
thingsToDo: Take the Salita Saraceni steps up La Rocca
Track down the rock-cut Lavatoio washhouse
Study the Christ Pantocrator mosaic in the Duomo
Cool off at the old-town beach at dusk
6.mooringTip: Reserve Porto Presidiana, the harbour on the northeast side of La Rocca; season transit space is tight, with three to five metres inside. In calm weather the old-town beach anchorage on sand works too, though it turns unworkable the moment a northwest swell rolls in.

7.shortDescription: One last day, 36 miles home along the Sicilian shore, with a swim under Capo Zafferano and Palermo's markets waiting at the finish.
7.description: No need to bolt out of Cefalù before breakfast; the run home is 36 miles and usually a soft morning motor-sail. Stay a mile off the beach, tick off Capo Plaia and the loading cranes at Termini Imerese, and line up a final swim under the limestone of Capo Zafferano. The little bay at Sant'Elia, the fishing hamlet on the cape's east side, does nicely for lunch and one last dip. Round into the Gulf of Palermo and Monte Pellegrino fills the view again, the city gathered at its foot. Get lines, fuel and the inventory squared away first, because Friday is when the whole fleet troops back and the fuel quay backs up. Then go ashore: Ballarò market in the Albergheria runs into the evening, so eat panelle and sfincione on your feet before a last look at the Quattro Canti and the cathedral.
thingsToDo: Swim beneath Capo Zafferano off Sant'Elia
Eat panelle and sfincione at Ballarò market
Cross the Quattro Canti to Palermo's cathedral
Refuel and run the inventory before handover
7.mooringTip: Villa Igiea and the Cala both handle the returning fleet; check which berth the base has assigned you the day before, then med-moor on lazy lines in four to six metres. Hit the fuel quay early on Friday, because it clogs up as everyone comes home at once.

portisco-long.metaDesc: A week-long sail from Portisco across the strait to Corsica and back, taking in Porto Cervo, La Maddalena, Bonifacio and Porto Vecchio.

1.shortDescription: A short six-mile hop north to warm up the crew, ending at the Costa Smeralda's glossiest marina and a sandy swim at Cala Granu.
1.description: Only six miles separate Portisco from Porto Cervo, so there's no rush. We usually slip the lines mid-morning, motor-sail up the coast and drop the hook in Cala Granu before lunch. The cove takes sand at five to seven metres and sits well out of any northerly, so it's a calm spot to check the anchor windlass and let the crew shake off travel legs. Porto Cervo itself was drawn up from scratch in 1962 under the Aga Khan, and it shows in the curved stonework of the Piazzetta. The marina charges like the wounded bull in August, so many crews swim, then anchor for the night and save the berth fee. Ashore, the boutique lanes and the aperitivo crowd at Phi Beach are worth an hour before the sun drops behind the granite.
thingsToDo: Swim off the sand at Cala Granu
Wander the Piazzetta's curved stonework
Sunset aperitivo at Phi Beach
Browse the boutique lanes after dark
1.mooringTip: Berths at Marina di Porto Cervo run €300-600 a night at the August peak, so book well ahead or skip it. Anchoring in Cala Granu on sand at five to seven metres is the budget alternative, though there's no shore power.

2.shortDescription: Twelve miles up into the La Maddalena park, where pink granite coves like Porto Massimo and Cala Francese ring water in every shade of green.
2.description: The run north into the Maddalena archipelago is where the trip starts to feel like Sardinia proper. Twelve miles of open water, then the islands close in and the colour changes underfoot. Porto Massimo, on the north side of La Maddalena, sits in a rough half-circle of pink rock that catches the light late in the day. We tend to anchor there for the swim and move on. Cala Francese, a little further round, is the better snorkel: the bottom shelves in bands of jade and deep blue, and the visibility on a settled day is genuinely good. One thing to sort before you arrive — the park requires mooring buoys to be booked online in July and August, and the good ones go early. Provision a plate of fregola with clams at a harbour osteria before the evening.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the jade shallows at Cala Francese
Anchor swim in Porto Massimo's pink coves
Kayak between the granite outcrops
Fregola with clams at a harbour osteria
2.mooringTip: Park mooring buoys at Porto Massimo and Cala Francese run €40-80 a night in peak season and must be reserved online through the Parco di La Maddalena. Book before you leave the dock, as the sheltered fields fill fast in August.

3.shortDescription: A gentle eight miles west to Santa Teresa Gallura, Sardinia's northern tip, with a white-sand beach in town and Corsica in plain sight.
3.description: Santa Teresa Gallura marks the top of the island, tucked into a natural inlet that shrugs off wind from any quarter. That makes it a reliable overnight even when the forecast looks messy for tomorrow's strait crossing. Rena Bianca, the town beach, is a short walk from the quay and shelves gently over pale sand, so it's an easy family swim. Climb the Longonsardo tower above the village at dusk.
