From the sixteenth-century Aragonese walls you look straight across the water to the white cliffs of Bonifacio, which is where you're headed next. Save an hour for the square, Piazza Vittorio Emanuele, and a small glass of mirto, the local myrtle liqueur that most bars pour cold. If there's wind for it, the granite chaos at Capo Testa is worth a lunch stop on the way in.
thingsToDo: Swim off Rena Bianca in town
Climb the Longonsardo tower at dusk
Mirto tasting on Piazza Vittorio Emanuele
Lunch stop among Capo Testa's granite
3.mooringTip: The marina at Santa Teresa Gallura takes you stern-to at €60-100 a night and stays calm in any wind thanks to the headland. If it's full, anchor off Rena Bianca on sand at four to six metres.

4.shortDescription: Eight miles across the notorious Bonifacio Strait into Corsica, ending in a cliff-cut harbour that hides inside a limestone fjord.
4.description: Short in miles, long in respect. The Bocche di Bonifacio funnels the Mistral between the two islands and can add five to eight knots to whatever the open sea is doing, which makes this the windiest stretch of the western Med. We only cross in daylight and only with a forecast under twenty-five knots; if it's blowing harder, we wait, and Santa Teresa is a fine place to wait. Get an early start, because the wind tends to build through the afternoon. Bonifacio pays you back on arrival. The harbour is a slot cut into a seventy-metre limestone cliff, and the marina sits deep inside the fjord where nothing can touch you. Ashore, the citadel walls, the King of Aragon's staircase down the rock face, and a bowl of wild-boar stew at U Castille fill an evening.
thingsToDo: Walk the upper-town citadel walls
Descend the King of Aragon's staircase
Tender into the Sdragonato sea cave
Wild-boar stew at U Castille
4.mooringTip: Marina de Bonifacio takes you stern-to at €100-180 a night in peak, fully sheltered deep in the fjord. Only attempt the strait in daylight with the Mistral forecast below twenty-five knots, and start early before the afternoon build.

5.shortDescription: Twenty miles up the Corsican east coast to Porto Vecchio, by way of Palombaggia's red rocks and the pines above powder-white sand.
5.description: The longest leg of the week, and the prettiest run of coast. Twenty miles northeast from Bonifacio brings you along a shoreline of umbrella pines and red granite, with Palombaggia the obvious stop before you reach port. Anchor off the beach on sand at four to six metres and go ashore for the powder underfoot; it earns its reputation on a calm morning. If the crew wants fish rather than sand, the Cerbicale islands sit a few miles offshore as a marine reserve, and the snorkelling over the reef edges is worth the detour. Porto Vecchio has been a working port since the Genoese built the citadel above the old salt pans in the sixteenth century. The upper town is a good evening wander, and a chestnut-flour beer from a Corsican brewery is the proper way to end the day.
thingsToDo: Anchor swim off Palombaggia beach
Snorkel the Cerbicale marine reserve
Walk the Genoese citadel's upper town
Chestnut-flour Corsican beer at day's end
5.mooringTip: Marina de Porto Vecchio takes you stern-to at €100-150 a night in peak and shelters well from the north. For a day stop, anchor off Palombaggia on sand at four to six metres, keeping clear of the swimming lines.

6.shortDescription: Fifteen miles south back over the strait to a pine-fringed Sardinian cove reachable only by boat, all pale sand and turquoise.
6.description: Back across the Bocche you go, this time southbound, timing the crossing for the calmer morning hours as before. Fifteen miles later you're back under the Italian courtesy flag and dropping into Cala Santa Maria, one of those coves in the La Maddalena park you can only reach on the water. Pines come right down to the sand, the bottom is clean at four to six metres, and the whole bay sits out of the northerlies. It's a snorkelling cove more than a walking one: parrotfish work the rocks at the edges, and there's usually an anemone or two if you look. We like to lay up here for the afternoon, swim, and eat a slice of fiadone, the Corsican lemon cheesecake, on deck. Remember the park buoys still need booking, even this late in the loop.
thingsToDo: Anchor swim in boat-only Cala Santa Maria
Snorkel the rock edges for parrotfish
Slice of fiadone on deck
Laze the afternoon in the pines
6.mooringTip: Cala Santa Maria's mooring buoys run €40-60 a night in peak and, like the rest of the park, must be pre-booked online. Aim to arrive by early afternoon, as the boat-only coves draw a crowd once the day-trip fleet moves in.

7.shortDescription: A final fifteen miles home to Portisco, stringing together the celebrity swim stops of Capriccioli and Cala di Volpe before the fuel dock.
7.description: The last day is built around swims rather than miles. Fifteen easy miles carry you south from the archipelago back toward Portisco, and there's no reason to hurry any of it. Capriccioli is the first stop, its beach broken up by rounded granite boulders that stand like sentinels off the sand. Then Cala di Volpe, the bay the Aga Khan set put on the map in the sixties and still full of superyacht hardware in season. Take a last long swim there. Liscia Ruja, just south, has warm shallow water and a beach lunch if the crew isn't ready to hand the boat back. When you are, top up at the fuel berth by the Portisco entrance before you tie up, and confirm your handover slot the day before so the morning goes smoothly.
thingsToDo: Anchor swim among Capriccioli's granite boulders
Final dip in celebrity Cala di Volpe
Beach lunch at warm-water Liscia Ruja
Refuel at the Portisco entrance berth
7.mooringTip: Marina di Portisco berths you stern-to on lazy lines at €120-200 a night in peak. Refuel at the fuel berth just inside the entrance, and lock in your handover time twenty-four hours ahead to avoid a queue at the pontoon.

portisco.metaDesc: Sail seven days round-trip from Portisco: Mortorio, Porto Rotondo, Golfo Aranci, Olbia and Tavolara. Anchorages, moorings and tables worth booking.

1.shortDescription: A gentle four-mile hop south to shake down the boat, then anchor off an empty granite islet for the season's first swim.
1.description: Portisco to Mortorio is barely an hour under power, which is the point on a first day. You want time to find where the winches jam and how the windlass behaves before any of it matters. Mortorio itself has no jetty, no bar, no residents at all, just pink granite and a few grazing gulls. We usually make for Cala dei Sassi on the north side, dropping onto sand in four to six metres where the holding is clean and the northerlies stay off your back. Swim across to the south shore and you find the little tidal pools the locals call the Pools of Venus, warm and clear over the rock. There is nothing to do here but that, which after a travel day is exactly enough. Lunch on deck, then head back before the light goes.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim at Cala dei Sassi
Snorkel the Pools of Venus tidal pools
Comb the shore for quartz sea-glass
Lunch on deck under the granite
1.mooringTip: Drop the hook for free on the sand off Cala dei Sassi, four to six metres and sheltered from any northerly. There's no overnight option here, though, so time your run back to Cala di Volpe or Portisco while the light holds.

2.shortDescription: Porto Rotondo lies a short leg north, its marble piazza and one of the Med's priciest marinas earned after a pink-granite swim.
2.description: Four miles again, so no rush to leave. Porto Rotondo was drawn up from scratch in the early sixties by the Dona dalle Rose family, and it shows: everything is marble underfoot and bougainvillaea overhead, a resort playing at being an old town. Stop first at Spiaggia Ira on the way in, a cove backed by pink granite where you can anchor on sand in five to seven metres and cool off before the marble takes over. The marina is genuinely one of the most expensive in the Mediterranean, well north of two hundred euros a night in August, so plenty of crews swing on the hook off Ira and dinghy in instead. Ashore, climb up to the Piazzetta with its giant chessboard, find Pietro Cascella's Circle of Life sculpture, and take an aperitivo while the yacht crowd parades past.
thingsToDo: Swim at Spiaggia Ira's pink-granite cove
Cross the Piazzetta's marble chessboard
Find the Circle of Life sculpture
Aperitivo among the Via dei Negozi shops
2.mooringTip: The marina takes you stern-to with lazy lines but charges two hundred to four hundred euros a night at peak. For a fraction of that, anchor off Spiaggia Ira on sand in five to seven metres and run the tender ashore.

3.shortDescription: Six miles east to a working little port at Sardinia's northeast corner, with a famous dive on an underwater peak a mile offshore.
3.description: Golfo Aranci sits at the very tip of the northeast headland, and it feels a world apart from the resort towns behind you, more fishing boats than superyachts. The marina is small and completely sheltered, which matters if the wind gets up overnight. Book ahead in August; the berths fill early. The reason to come lies a mile east of the entrance, the Secca di Capo Figari, an underwater mountain that rises toward the surface and pulls in groupers, barracuda and the odd amberjack. Fly a dive flag if you plan to go down. Back on land, the walk out to the Capo Figari lighthouse repays the effort with the whole gulf laid out below. Come evening, crews tend to drift toward the quayside tables for a plate of lobster linguine and a slow sunset.
thingsToDo: Dive or snorkel the Secca di Capo Figari
Walk the Capo Figari lighthouse trail
Sunset drinks at Rosa dei Venti
Lobster linguine at Ristorante Da Paolo
3.mooringTip: Berth stern-to with lazy lines in the marina, roughly one hundred to one hundred fifty euros a night in season. It is fully sheltered, but call ahead in August because the small basin books out fast.

4.shortDescription: Eight miles south into Olbia, a real Sardinian town with a Roman past, a Romanesque church and the best provisioning stop of the week.
4.description: After a run of resorts and empty islands, Olbia is a proper town, and a good place to top up the boat and the fridge. The marina has full services and a fuel berth at the entrance, so take on diesel on your way in. Olbia's story runs deep: the archaeological museum lays out its Phoenician and Roman layers, and the little Basilica di San Simplicio, built from the same granite the coast is made of, has stood since the Middle Ages. If the crew wants a beach, it is a short cycle out to Pittulongu, sand and shallow water sheltered from the north. Save your appetite for the covered market, where the porceddu, slow-roasted suckling pig, is the thing to order. Stock up properly here, because the shops thin out fast on the quieter southern leg.
thingsToDo: Tour the Phoenician-Roman archaeological museum
See the granite Basilica di San Simplicio
Cycle out to Pittulongu beach
Porceddu suckling pig at the covered market
4.mooringTip: Olbia Marina is stern-to with lazy lines and full services, around one hundred to one hundred fifty euros a night at peak. Take on fuel at the entrance berth as you come in rather than fighting for it on the way out.

5.shortDescription: A longer twelve-mile leg south to a fishing village facing Tavolara, with a seagrass snorkel stop and a climb for the view on the way.
5.description: This is the longest hop of the week, though twelve miles is still an easy morning. Worth leaving by nine if you want to break the passage at Cala Girgolu, where the anchorage sits over seagrass meadows that draw fish in close for a snorkel. Ashore, the climb up Monte Coda Cavallo is short and steep, and the payoff is Tavolara filling the horizon like a stranded ship. Porto San Paolo is small and unpretentious, a village that still smells of fish rather than money. The marina is modest and cheap by Costa Smeralda standards, tucked from the northerlies. This is bottarga country, cured grey-mullet roe shaved over pasta, and a handful of families still cure it by hand nearby. Ask at a quayside trattoria and someone will point you to a workshop.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the seagrass at Cala Girgolu
Climb Monte Coda Cavallo for Tavolara
Bottarga shaved over pasta on the quay
Visit a family bottarga workshop
5.mooringTip: The village marina takes you stern-to at a friendly sixty to ninety euros a night, well sheltered from the north. If it is full, anchor back at Cala Girgolu on sand in four to six metres and tender in.

6.shortDescription: An uninhabited island of wild goats and old ruins sits four miles across, with an empty south-coast cove for the swim.
6.description: Molara lies just east of Tavolara, part of the same protected waters, and almost nobody lives on it. Wild goats work the slopes and the ground is scattered with old ruins, medieval and older, for anyone who fancies a scramble ashore. Round to the south coast and drop the hook off Cala Spagnola, where the water runs clear over sand and the granite shoreline rewards a slow snorkel. There is no quay and no overnight here, so treat it as a long lunch stop. Pack pane carasau, the crisp Sardinian flatbread that keeps well aboard, along with whatever you bought in Olbia. When the light softens, pull the anchor and run the four miles back to Porto San Paolo, or carry on to Tavolara's Spalmatore spit to tie up for the night.
thingsToDo: Swim off Cala Spagnola's sandy cove
Snorkel the granite shoreline
Scramble up to the island ruins
Picnic on pane carasau flatbread
6.mooringTip: Anchoring is free on sand at four to six metres, sheltered from the north, but it is strictly a day stop. Plan to overnight back at Porto San Paolo or at Spalmatore on Tavolara before the light goes.

7.shortDescription: The long fifteen-mile run home, with a last swim off Tavolara and a snorkel over one of the Med's finest reefs before you hand back.
7.description: Fifteen miles north closes the loop, so leave in good time and build in the stops. Tavolara's Spalmatore di Terra, the flat sandy spit at the island's foot, makes a fine last swim, with a couple of beach tavernas if you fancy lunch off the boat. Just offshore lies the Secca del Papa, regularly rated among the best dives in the Mediterranean, its pinnacles dressed in red gorgonian; even snorkelling the top of it sticks in the memory. From there it is a straight run back to Portisco. Refuel at the berth by the entrance before you take your slot, and give the boat a rinse while the light lasts. A glass of mirto, the island's myrtle liqueur, is the traditional way to close a Sardinian week. Confirm your handover time the day before.
thingsToDo: Last swim at Spalmatore di Terra, Tavolara
Snorkel the Secca del Papa reef
Mirto myrtle liqueur to finish
Refuel and rinse down at Marina di Portisco
7.mooringTip: Marina di Portisco berths you stern-to with lazy lines, roughly one hundred twenty to two hundred euros a night at peak. Refuel at the entrance berth before mooring and confirm your handover slot twenty-four hours ahead.

salerno-south.metaDesc: Sail seven days south from Salerno into the Cilento National Park: Cetara, Agropoli, Acciaroli, Palinuro and the wild Infreschi marine reserve.

1.shortDescription: An easy eight-mile hop west from Marina d'Arechi to Cetara, the little Amalfi port that turned anchovies into liquid gold.
1.description: The first leg is deliberately short, and I like it that way. Eight miles out of Marina d'Arechi puts you off Cetara well before lunch, with the rest of the day to find your feet. This is the working end of the Amalfi Coast: a wedge of tall houses stacked above a fishing harbour, a majolica-domed church, and the squat Torre Vicereale guarding the beach. Cetara made its name with colatura di alici, the amber anchovy sauce descended from Roman garum, and the families who press it still work cellars a few steps from the quay. Order spaghetti alla colatura at a harbour trattoria and you understand the fuss. Late afternoon, climb toward the tower for the light on the water, then settle in with a limoncello as the boats come back.
thingsToDo: Visit a colatura di alici cellar by the harbour
Spaghetti alla colatura at a quayside trattoria
Climb to the Torre Vicereale watchtower
Majolica-tiled dome of San Pietro Apostolo
1.mooringTip: Cetara's little harbour takes you stern-to at roughly €40-60 a night and sits well tucked from northerlies, but it fills fast in July and August, so raft in by mid-afternoon or call ahead. If it's full, Marina d'Arechi eight miles back has the berths and full services.

2.shortDescription: Twenty miles south across the Gulf of Salerno to Agropoli, gateway to the Cilento park and the Greek temples of Paestum.
2.description: This is the day the coast changes character. You leave the vertical drama of Amalfi behind and cross open water toward the softer, greener shoulder of the Cilento. Twenty miles is a proper sail if the westerly fills in by early afternoon, so we usually slip the lines by nine. Agropoli announces itself with an Angevin-Aragonese castle riding the headland above the old town, its lanes climbing to a gate and a view back over the gulf. The real prize sits inland: Paestum, where three Doric temples have stood since the sixth century BC, a short taxi from the marina. Closer to hand, Trentova Bay a mile north gives clear water over sand for an afternoon swim. Provision here too; the buffalo-mozzarella farms of the Sele plain are the best you'll taste all week.
thingsToDo: Taxi to the Doric temples at Paestum
Swim over sand in Trentova Bay
Climb to the Angevin-Aragonese castle
Buffalo mozzarella from a Sele-plain farm
2.mooringTip: Marina di Agropoli takes you stern-to for about €40-70, sheltered from the north; book ahead in high summer as the pontoons are popular. For a lunch stop, drop the hook in Trentova Bay on sand in four to six metres and swim off the boat.

3.shortDescription: Fifteen miles down the Cilento shore to Acciaroli, a whitewashed fishing port with a Hemingway legend and Slow Food anchovies.
3.description: Acciaroli is small, low and white, wrapped around one of the prettiest little harbours on this coast. A Norman tower stands over the entrance and the church of the Annunziata anchors the piazza behind it. Local lore has Hemingway putting in here in the early fifties, and the village has run with the story ever since; take it with a smile and a glass of something cold. What is beyond doubt is the fishing. The anchovies here are still caught with menaica nets, a Slow Food method that keeps the fish whole and sweet, and you'll find them baked with oregano at the quay trattorias. Three miles on lies Pioppi, where the American physiologist Ancel Keys spent decades documenting what the world now calls the Mediterranean diet. Its little museum is worth the walk.
thingsToDo: Menaica anchovies baked with oregano at the quay
Walk to the Norman tower and Annunziata church
Pioppi's Mediterranean Diet museum, three miles south
Swim off the harbour breakwater at dusk
3.mooringTip: Acciaroli's harbour is stern-to and cheap by Amalfi standards, around €30-50, and stays calm in northerlies, but space is tight, so aim to arrive before five. Prefer a swim day? Anchor off Pioppi on sand in four to six metres instead.

4.shortDescription: A short eight-mile leg to Marina di Casalvelino, at the foot of ancient Elea, where Parmenides argued philosophy into being.
4.description: Only eight miles today, which leaves the afternoon free for something unusual on a sailing holiday: ancient philosophy. Just inland from Marina di Casalvelino lie the ruins of Elea, the Greek colony the Romans called Velia, home to Parmenides and Zeno and their school of hair-splitting logic. Walk up through the site to the Porta Rosa, an arch older than most of what survives in Italy, and the centuries fall away. Back at sea level, the long sweep of Ascea beach runs off to the south, backed by pines and easy to reach by tender for a swim over clean sand. Evenings here are quiet farming-village affairs. Track down a family kitchen serving hand-rolled fusilli cilentani under wild-boar ragù, pour a Cilento red, and you've had a good day.
thingsToDo: Walk the ruins of Elea to Porta Rosa
Swim off Ascea's pine-backed sand beach
Fusilli cilentani with wild-boar ragù inland
Sunset drink on the Casalvelino quay
4.mooringTip: Marina di Casalvelino is a tidy little berth, stern-to at roughly €40-60 and protected from the north; it's small, so a call ahead in season saves surprises. For lunch, anchor off Ascea on sand in four to six metres and dinghy ashore.

5.shortDescription: Fifteen miles to Capo Palinuro, the Cilento's great limestone headland riddled with sea caves and crowned by one of Italy's tallest lighthouses.
5.description: Palinuro is the set-piece of this coast, and you feel it coming: a great pale headland shouldering out into deep blue, honeycombed with caves at the waterline. Named for the helmsman Aeneas lost here in the Aeneid, the cape hides more than thirty grottoes. The Grotta Azzurra is the one everyone wants, its water lit an electric blue where sun slips through an underwater window; take the tender in on a calm morning before the tour boats arrive. Round to the south side and you'll find the Arco Naturale standing offshore and Cala del Buon Dormire tucked below the cliffs for a swim. Ashore, the harbour trattorias do pezzogna all'acqua pazza, local bream poached with tomato and garlic. Fit crews can hike the trail up to the Capo Palinuro lighthouse for the long view south.
thingsToDo: Tender into the Grotta Azzurra at dawn
Swim below the cliffs at Cala del Buon Dormire
Photograph the offshore Arco Naturale
Pezzogna all'acqua pazza at the harbour
Hike to the Capo Palinuro lighthouse
5.mooringTip: Marina di Palinuro sits on the sheltered east side and takes you stern-to at about €50-80, calm in northerlies; it's the busiest stop of the week, so book. Day-anchor off the south-side coves like Cala del Buon Dormire, but move round for the night.

6.shortDescription: Just eight miles to Marina di Camerota and the wild, boat-only coves of the Infreschi marine reserve, the emptiest water on the route.
6.description: The shortest hop of the week drops you at the edge of the best-protected coast in Campania. South of Marina di Camerota the shore turns to cliff and juniper, and boat traffic thins to almost nothing inside the Infreschi and Masseta reserve. Baia degli Infreschi is the headline: a near-enclosed bay of pale sand and green water reachable only from the sea or on foot, named for the cold springs that seep up along its shore. Anchor early, because the day-boats find it too. A little further sits Cala Bianca, a bright ribbon of shingle that once topped a national poll for Italy's finest beach. Camerota itself keeps a proper fishing-village feel; sit on the dock over sea urchins and a cold Fiano, and let the afternoon go long.
thingsToDo: Anchor in Baia degli Infreschi early
Swim the shingle at Cala Bianca
Sea urchins and Fiano on the dock
Coastal path around the Infreschi reserve
6.mooringTip: Marina di Camerota takes you stern-to at roughly €40-60 and shelters you from the north. Baia degli Infreschi is a day-anchorage only, on sand in four to six metres, with no overnight allowed inside the reserve, so plan to be back on the pontoon by evening.

7.shortDescription: The long haul home: thirty-eight miles north to Salerno, with a last swim off the Castellabate watchtower before the handover.
7.description: The final day is the big one, thirty-eight miles back up the coast to Salerno, so this is a morning for an early start and a fair forecast. With the summer breeze usually behind you on the way north, it can be a genuinely lovely sail rather than a slog. Break it halfway at San Marco di Castellabate, where a sixteenth-century watchtower stands above a sand beach and the submerged stones of an ancient Greek port lie just off it; drop the hook, swim, and eat something before the last stretch. Aim to reach Marina d'Arechi with light to spare, topping up at the fuel berth by the entrance before you tie up. Then walk Salerno's palm-lined Lungomare at sunset with a sfogliatella, and let the trip settle before tomorrow's inventory.
thingsToDo: Swim below the San Marco di Castellabate watchtower
Snorkel the submerged ancient Greek port
Refuel and clean up at Marina d'Arechi
Sfogliatella on Salerno's Lungomare Trieste
7.mooringTip: Marina d'Arechi is a full-service marina and stern-to, but peak nights run €100-160, so it's the priciest stop; reserve your return berth when you book the charter. Take on fuel at the entrance berth before you go looking for your pontoon.

salerno.metaDesc: Round-trip from Salerno: sail Vietri, Maiori, Amalfi and Sorrento, then cross the Bay of Naples to Ischia before looping back via Cetara. Seven days.

1.shortDescription: A short four-mile hop west to ease into the charter, then an afternoon among Vietri's ceramic workshops above the old harbour.
1.description:
The first leg barely counts as a passage. Four miles out of Marina d'Arechi, hugging the coast, and you're off Vietri sul Mare before anyone has finished their coffee. That's the point: a gentle warm-up to check the reefing lines and the tender before the real distances begin.

Vietri is where Amalfi ceramics begin. Walk up from the little marina and the Corso is stacked with small studios, most still firing their own kilns out the back. One of the classic local motifs is the ciucciariello, a stubby painted donkey you'll see on everything from egg cups to house numbers. Ask and a potter will often let you throw a small piece yourself.

We usually tie up early and spend the whole afternoon ashore. Come evening, a plate of scialatielli and a glass of Falanghina on the quay is about right.
thingsToDo: Throw your own bowl at a Corso studio
Find the majolica dome of San Giovanni Battista
Scialatielli with seafood at a harbour trattoria
Evening gelato along the seafront walk
1.mooringTip: The little Marina di Vietri takes you stern-to for around €40-60 and shelters you from the northerlies, but berths are few and fill by mid-afternoon in season. If it's full, Marina d'Arechi four miles back has proper services and always has room.

2.shortDescription: Eight miles down the coast to Maiori's kilometre of sand, with a lunch swim off Minori and lemon delizie from Sal De Riso.
2.description:
Maiori has the one thing the rest of this coast lacks: real sand, close to a kilometre of it, running flat behind a proper breakwater. After the vertical villages further west, it feels almost roomy. The marina sits inside the mole, so you can walk straight off the pontoon into a beach town that never got too glossy.

Break the passage at Minori, a mile short. Drop the hook on sand in four to six metres and swim before lunch while the anchorage is still quiet. Ashore, Sal De Riso is the reason people come: the delizia al limone, a dome of sponge and lemon cream built on the local sfusato, is worth the queue.

Between the two towns runs the old Sentiero dei Limoni, a stepped path through terraced groves. Worth doing early, before the sun gets on it.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim off Minori's sand
Queue for a delizia al limone at Sal De Riso
Walk the Sentiero dei Limoni to Minori
See the Roman villa ruins at Minori
2.mooringTip: Maiori's marina berths you stern-to inside the breakwater for roughly €70-120 and holds well against the prevailing northerlies. Use Minori only as a daytime anchorage on sand in four to six metres; it's open to the south and no place to spend the night.

3.shortDescription: Six miles to Amalfi itself, with a tender run into the Emerald Grotto at Conca and the striped cathedral waiting on the piazza.
3.description:
This is the town the whole coast is named for, and it wears it well. The harbour is small and gets busy, so we like to be alongside by late morning before the day boats swing in. From the quay it's a two-minute walk to Piazza Duomo and the long flight of steps up to the Cathedral of Sant'Andrea, its front a bold stripe of Arab-Norman stonework.

Amalfi made its money on paper and on the sea. The Museo della Carta, up the valley in an old mill, still turns rag into sheets on medieval gear.

On the way in, detour to Conca dei Marini and tender into the Grotta dello Smeraldo, where light comes up through a submerged opening and turns the water green. Go mid-morning; the colour flattens once the sun climbs.
thingsToDo: Climb to the Cathedral of Sant'Andrea
See the Cloister of Paradise beside the Duomo
Watch paper made at the Museo della Carta
Tender into the Grotta dello Smeraldo
3.mooringTip: Porto di Amalfi is tight and priced to match, up to €100-180 in July and August, so book the berth days ahead rather than hoping for a gap. If nothing's free you can anchor outside on sand in six to eight metres and run the tender in, weather permitting.

4.shortDescription: Twelve miles round Punta Campanella to Sorrento, passing Positano's buoys and the Li Galli islands, with the Path of the Gods above.
4.description:
The longest run of the west coast, and the prettiest. Leave the mooring by nine and you can afford to slow down off Positano, where the town stacks up the hillside in ochre and pink. There's a buoy field below the beach; pick one up, swim, and have lunch aboard rather than fight for a table ashore.

Past Positano the Li Galli islands sit low off the point, once owned by the dancer Rudolf Nureyev. Round Punta Campanella and the water changes character as you leave the Amalfi side for the Sorrentine peninsula.

Marina Piccola di Sorrento is a ten-minute climb below the old town. Book a table at Donna Sofia for gnocchi alla Sorrentina, then take the sunset from the terrace of the Foreigners' Club above the harbour.
thingsToDo: Swim off Positano from a lunch buoy
Gnocchi alla Sorrentina at Donna Sofia
Aperitivo on the Foreigners' Club terrace
Wander Sorrento's Marina Grande fishing quarter
4.mooringTip: Marina Piccola takes you stern-to for around €100-160 in high season and sits well tucked from the northerly winds. It's open to the south, though, so if a libeccio is forecast overnight, ask the marineros for an inside berth or move on.

5.shortDescription: Twenty miles north across the Bay of Naples to Mergellina, with pizza in Spaccanapoli and a Caravaggio a short walk from the marina.
5.description:
Twenty miles of open bay, usually a straightforward reach with Vesuvius filling the view off the starboard bow the whole way. If the crew wants a last swim before the city, drop in at Marechiaro on the Posillipo shore, a fishermen's cove that gave Naples one of its old songs.

Mergellina is the practical berth, central and well connected, with Castel dell'Ovo and the Lungomare a kilometre east along the waterfront. Naples rewards walking more than it rewards a marina. Head up into Spaccanapoli, the dead-straight old street that cuts the historic centre, and eat a margherita at Sorbillo where the queue is part of the ritual.

Ten minutes off that street, Pio Monte della Misericordia still hangs Caravaggio's Seven Works of Mercy in the church it was painted for.
thingsToDo: Swim at Marechiaro before the city
Margherita at Sorbillo on Via dei Tribunali
Caravaggio at Pio Monte della Misericordia
Evening walk the Lungomare to Castel dell'Ovo
5.mooringTip: Mergellina berths you stern-to for around €80-130 and is protected from the north, with taxis and the metro close by. For a better position under Castel dell'Ovo, try Borgo Marinari instead; there are fewer slots, so call ahead in season.

6.shortDescription: Fifteen miles west to volcanic Ischia, mooring at Casamicciola for its thermal springs, a soak at Poseidon and the climb up Monte Epomeo.
6.description:
Ischia is the bigger, greener, more lived-in cousin of Capri, and it runs on hot water. The whole island is volcanic, and springs feed thermal parks and bays all the way round. Casamicciola Terme on the north coast is the old spa harbour and the handiest place to leave the boat, though Forio and little Sant'Angelo on the south make good alternatives if the wind sits wrong.

Spend the afternoon at the Giardini Poseidon above Forio, a hillside of thermal pools at different temperatures, day pass about €35. If the crew has legs left, the walk up Monte Epomeo takes a couple of hours and opens a view back over the whole bay to Capri.

Eat coniglio all'ischitana, the island's slow rabbit stew, at a vineyard trattoria inland.
thingsToDo: Soak the pools at Giardini Poseidon
Hike Monte Epomeo for the bay view
Coniglio all'ischitana at an inland vineyard
Swim the hot springs at Sorgeto bay
6.mooringTip: Casamicciola berths you stern-to for roughly €60-100, handy for the thermal port but open to the north. If a tramontana is forecast, shift round to Forio or onto the buoys off Sant'Angelo on the island's sheltered southern side.

7.shortDescription: A long thirty-two miles back to Salerno, breaking for lunch at Cetara, home of colatura anchovy sauce, before the handover at Marina d'Arechi.
7.description:
The last day is the big one: thirty-two miles back down the coast, so we like to slip the lines by eight and settle into the passage while it's still cool. It's a long reach past the whole Amalfi shoreline you sailed out along, seen this time from further offshore.

Cetara makes the natural lunch stop. It's a working fishing village, less made-up than its neighbours, and it still produces colatura di alici, the amber anchovy sauce that descends straight from Roman garum. Order spaghetti dressed with it at a harbour trattoria; a few drops carry the whole plate.

From Cetara it's a short hop to Marina d'Arechi. Refuel at the entrance berth, give the boat a rinse, and hand her back with the Lungomare and an aperitivo waiting.
thingsToDo: Spaghetti alla colatura at a Cetara trattoria
Visit a colatura di alici workshop
Refuel at the Marina d'Arechi fuel berth
Farewell aperitivo on the Lungomare Trieste
7.mooringTip: Marina d'Arechi is the full-service base and takes you stern-to for around €100-160 in peak weeks, with fuel, water and easy road access for the handover. Top up at the fuel berth by the entrance before you go to your slip, not the morning you leave in a hurry.

sorento.metaTitle: 7-Day Amalfi to Cilento by Sail | Boat4You
sorento.metaDesc: A 7-day sail from Sorrento round Punta Campanella to Positano and Amalfi, then south into wild Cilento for Agropoli, Acciaroli and Paestum.

1.shortDescription: An easy 12-mile first leg from Sorrento around Punta Campanella to Positano, passing the private Li Galli islets on the way in.
1.description: Pick up your boat at Marina Piccola and take the morning to sort gear before slipping the lines. The run west and then south is short, but Punta Campanella is a marine reserve where the tide can push hard against the headland, so we usually round it with a bit of throttle in hand. Once you are clear, the coast opens up and Positano climbs the hillside ahead of you. Three miles out sit the Li Galli islets, the old Sirens' rocks, private and worth a slow pass. Positano runs on buoys, not berths. Call the harbour master early to hold one off Spiaggia Grande, and if the field is full, tuck west into Fornillo and drop the hook on sand. Evenings here are for the cliff stairs and a cold lemon delizia.
thingsToDo: Climb the stairs to Santa Maria Assunta church
Swim off Fornillo beach before the crowds
Cold lemon delizia at a cliff-top bar
Walk a stretch of the Path of the Gods
1.mooringTip: Positano is buoys only, roughly €80–150 a night in peak, so phone the harbour master ahead rather than turning up hopeful. Fornillo Bay is the fallback, good holding on sand in 6–8 m, sheltered from the north but open to a southerly libeccio.

2.shortDescription: A short six-mile hop east under the cliffs to Praiano, with the Emerald Grotto at Conca dei Marini the reason to slow down.
2.description: Barely six miles today, so there is no rush. We tend to leave late, swim somewhere quiet, and time the arrival at Conca dei Marini for early afternoon when the light is right. The Emerald Grotto sits below the road here, a flooded sea cave where sunlight comes up through an underwater gap and turns the whole chamber green. Take the tender in and go slowly; the entrance is low. Praiano itself has only Marina di Praia, a scrap of a fishing harbour wedged into a cleft, so plan to anchor outside on sand rather than squeeze in. Ashore, the village is all cliff stairs and small belvederes looking back toward Positano. Dinner is easy: Il Monastero does a zucchini-blossom risotto worth the climb up from the water.
thingsToDo: Tender into the Emerald Grotto sea cave
Snorkel the rocks off Marina di Praia
Zucchini-blossom risotto at Il Monastero
Sunset from a Praiano cliff belvedere
2.mooringTip: Drop the hook outside Marina di Praia on sand in 6–8 m; it holds well and shelters you from the north. The buoys at Conca dei Marini are for daytime grotto visits only, not an overnight.

3.shortDescription: Eight miles east to Amalfi and Maiori, with a medieval cathedral, a working paper mill and the coast's longest sand beach.
3.description: Amalfi was a sea republic long before it was a postcard, and it still keeps a small harbour under the cliffs. Book the berth ahead in July and August, because Porto di Amalfi fills fast and there is not much slack. Once you are tied up, the town pulls you straight up the steps to the cathedral of Sant'Andrea, all striped stone and a staircase that earns the view. Behind it, the old paper mill still turns its water wheels; the Museo della Carta shows how the coast made its famous rag paper. If Amalfi is booked out, run two miles on to Maiori, which has a proper marina behind its breakwater and a full kilometre of sand, rare on this shore. Close the day with a lemon granita along the front.
thingsToDo: Climb to the Cathedral of Sant'Andrea
Tour the Museo della Carta paper mill
Ride the Lemon Trail through sfusato groves
Lemon granita on the Maiori seafront
3.mooringTip: Porto di Amalfi is stern-to at around €100–180 in season and needs booking well ahead. Maiori's marina behind the breakwater is the roomier fallback at roughly €70–120, and easier to slip into late.

4.shortDescription: Another eight miles east brings Vietri, the coast's ceramics town, with big-marina comforts at Salerno's Marina d'Arechi just beyond.
4.description: This is where the Amalfi cliffs finally relax and the plain around Salerno opens out. Vietri sul Mare is the ceramics town, and you smell the kilns before you see them; the workshops line the Corso from the water up into the village, painted tiles on every step. Buy a hand-thrown pulcinella from a family studio and watch it get wrapped for the passage home. For the night, most crews carry on to Marina d'Arechi, four miles down at Salerno, the biggest marina in Campania with fuel, water and a proper chandlery. If you would rather stay close and old-fashioned, little Porto Vietri takes a handful of boats at half the price. Eat scialatielli with seafood at Mamma Rosa before turning in.
thingsToDo: Buy a hand-painted pulcinella from a Vietri studio
Walk the ceramic-tiled steps of the Corso
Scialatielli with seafood at Mamma Rosa
Wander Salerno's Giardino della Minerva
4.mooringTip: Marina d'Arechi is full-service stern-to at about €100–160 in peak and the practical overnight before the long jump south. Porto Vietri is smaller and historic at roughly €40–60 if you want to stay under the ceramics town.

5.shortDescription: A 20-mile run south out of the Amalfi world into the Cilento park, ending under Agropoli's castle near the temples of Paestum.
5.description: Today the scenery changes completely. You leave the vertical Amalfi coast behind and cross the Gulf of Salerno into Cilento, a lower, wilder, greener shore that most charter crews never reach. Twenty miles gets you to Agropoli, an old fishing town with an Aragonese castle standing on the headland above the harbour. Just north lies Trentova Bay, part of a marine reserve, where the water runs over seagrass meadows and the snorkelling is genuinely good. The reason to stop, though, is inland. Grab a taxi eight kilometres to Paestum and stand among three Greek temples that have held their columns since the sixth century before Christ. They put the whole coast in perspective. Back aboard, buy buffalo mozzarella straight from a Cilento farm; it never tastes the same anywhere else.
thingsToDo: Taxi to the Greek temples of Paestum
Snorkel Trentova Bay's seagrass meadows
Climb to the Aragonese castle
Buffalo mozzarella fresh from a Cilento farm
5.mooringTip: Agropoli's town quay is stern-to at roughly €40–70 and well sheltered from the north. If it is busy or blowing, the modern Marina di Agropoli inside the breakwater has more room and more services.

6.shortDescription: Fifteen miles further south to Acciaroli, Hemingway's old summer harbour, with a swim at the Sirenuse spires and a Pioppi taverna dinner.
6.description: Fifteen more miles down the Cilento shore brings you to Acciaroli, a small working port that still smells of diesel and fish rather than perfume. Hemingway spent the summer of 1952 here, and the locals will tell you he began The Old Man and the Sea looking at this water; whether that is true hardly matters once you have walked the front. Offshore the Sirenuse rocks break the surface in sharp spires, and the anchoring swim around them is the best of the trip. Three miles on sits Pioppi, the quiet village where a visiting scientist first pinned down what we now call the Mediterranean diet. It is a fitting place for dinner: pull in for grilled fish straight off the boats at a quay taverna, and cycle the little seafront trail if there is light left.
thingsToDo: Swim the Sirenuse rock spires at anchor
Walk the Hemingway waterfront in Acciaroli
Grilled fish at a Pioppi quay taverna
Cycle the Pioppi seafront diet trail
6.mooringTip: Acciaroli takes you stern-to in the harbour at around €30–50, sheltered from the north and rarely full. For a swim-and-lunch stop, anchor off Pioppi on sand in 4–6 m instead.

7.shortDescription: The long 38-mile passage home to Sorrento, back round Punta Campanella, with a last swim at the Li Galli islets if time allows.
7.description: The last day is a proper passage, so it is worth leaving by nine. Thirty-eight miles up the Cilento coast and back across the gulf is a full day under sail if the breeze cooperates, and a steady motor-sail if it does not. Keep an eye on Punta Campanella again as you round it; the current sets through the gap between the mainland and Capri and can knock a knot off your speed. If you are ahead of schedule, drop anchor on the south side of the Li Galli islets for a final swim in deep blue water and a plate of linguine alle vongole in the cockpit. From there it is a short hop into Marina Piccola. Refuel at the fuel berth by the entrance, rinse the salt off, and hand the boat back clean the next morning.
thingsToDo: Final swim off the Li Galli islets
Linguine alle vongole in the cockpit
Refuel at the Marina Piccola fuel berth
Farewell crew dinner on the Sorrento front
7.mooringTip: Marina Piccola di Sorrento is stern-to at about €100–160 in peak; confirm your handover slot 24 hours ahead so the berth is held. Top up fuel at the entrance berth on the way in to save a scramble at handover.

aeolian-full-circle-14-days.metaDesc: Two weeks from Portorosa around all seven Aeolian Islands, from Vulcano's mud baths to Stromboli's lava, then back via Cefalù and Palermo.

1.shortDescription: Slip the lines at Portorosa and point north for the sulphur-scented crater island where the Aeolians properly begin.
1.description: The first leg is a warm-up, roughly fifteen miles of open water once you clear the shoals off Portorosa. We usually leave by nine, before the afternoon breeze fills in from the northwest and kicks up a short chop across the channel. Vulcano announces itself long before you see it clearly; the smell of rotten eggs drifts downwind from the fumaroles. Drop the hook in Porto di Ponente on the west side, where the sand holds well in five to ten metres and the sunsets are worth the sulphur. Ashore, the mud baths near Porto di Levante are a rite of passage, though your swimwear may never recover. If your legs still have anything left, the Gran Cratere climb takes about an hour and the rim view over the whole archipelago sets up the fortnight ahead.
thingsToDo: Soak in the Porto di Levante mud baths
Climb the Gran Cratere rim (about an hour)
Watch sunset from Porto di Ponente's black sand
Swim over the warm underwater fumaroles
1.mooringTip: Porto di Ponente is the calm choice in easterlies; anchor in 5 to 10 metres on sand and give the cliffs a wide berth for submerged rocks. Levante has buoys, but the sulphur haze works its way into everything, so most crews prefer to sleep on the Ponente side.

2.shortDescription: A short morning hop to the archipelago's lively capital, with a Spanish citadel above the harbour and pumice cliffs to the north.
2.description: Barely an hour under sail separates Vulcano from Lipari, so there is no rush. Slide up the east coast and the old town rises ahead, the castle walls of the citadel catching the morning light above Marina Corta. Tie up or grab a buoy, then wander into the lanes behind the quay where the archaeological museum lays out four thousand years of island history in plain terms. Lipari earns a full day. Hire a scooter and run north past Canneto to the old pumice quarries at Porticello, where the white spoil still turns the shallows a milky turquoise. Come evening, Via Garibaldi fills with the smell of grilled fish and the local Malvasia gets poured freely. We often stay two nights here when the schedule allows; the island earns them.
thingsToDo: Tour the citadel's archaeological museum
Scooter to Porticello's white pumice shallows
Aperitivo along Via Garibaldi at dusk
Swim below the Canneto pumice cliffs
2.mooringTip: Marina Corta is postcard-pretty but tight and often full by late afternoon; Marina Lunga (Pignataro) to the north has more room and better shelter from the northwest. Book a berth ahead in July and August, or anchor south of Marina Corta in about 12 metres on sand.

3.shortDescription: Cross to the greenest Aeolian for Malvasia vineyards, caper terraces and the cliff-ringed bay where Il Postino was filmed.
3.description: Salina sits a couple of hours northwest, twin volcanic cones green with vines where the other islands run to bare rock. Santa Marina makes an easy landfall, a proper little marina with water and fuel and a walkable seafront. The island rewards a car more than most. Drive up to Malfa and out to Pollara, the half-collapsed crater bay where the fishermen still pull their boats up the shingle. Salina takes its food seriously. Capers grow wild on the terraces, the Malvasia delle Lipari is honeyed and worth a case in the bilge, and every bar claims the definitive granita. We rate the ones in Santa Marina for breakfast, brioche and all. Sunset from Pollara, with Filicudi floating on the horizon, is the sort of thing crews talk about for years.
thingsToDo: Taste Malvasia at a Malfa winery
Swim in the Pollara crater bay
Order caper-laden pasta in Santa Marina
Try the granita and brioche at breakfast
3.mooringTip: Santa Marina's marina is the practical base with full services; call ahead in high season. If the northwest is blowing hard, Rinella on the south coast offers a quieter buoy field and a black-sand beach, though it fills fast with day boats.

4.shortDescription: Head out to the wild western edge of the chain, where a sea cave, a lone rock spire and a fishing-village terrace wait.
4.description: Filicudi is the quiet one, a longer reach west across open water where the swell has room to build, so pick a settled forecast. Round the south of the island and the Grotta del Bue Marino opens in the cliff, a sea cave you can nose a tender into when it is calm. Off the western tip stands La Canna, a needle of rock climbing seventy metres straight out of the sea. Anchor off Filicudi Porto or drop into the bay below Pecorini a Mare. Ashore, the terrace at La Sirena in Pecorini is the reason many people sail this far, the chef cooking whatever the fishermen landed that morning a few steps away. There is almost nothing to do here beyond swim, eat and watch the light change. That is rather the point.
thingsToDo: Tender into the Grotta del Bue Marino
Circle La Canna's seventy-metre rock spire
Lunch on the terrace at La Sirena
Snorkel the clear water off Capo Graziano
4.mooringTip: Filicudi Porto has a small pier with a handful of buoys and limited depth, so arrive early. The bay off Pecorini a Mare gives good holding on sand but opens to the south; if any southerly is forecast, move round to the more sheltered eastern side for the night.

5.shortDescription: Retrace east toward Salina with time for a swim stop, closing the western loop before the islands' glamorous heart.
5.description: The return to Salina is the same water in reverse, but a different day. With the wind usually behind you on an easterly heading, it is often the easiest sail of the trip, so there is no need to leave at dawn. Take the long way if the sea is kind and pass close under Filicudi's north face, where the terraced walls climb impossibly steep above the water. Halfway across, heave to for a swim where the seabed drops away deep and blue beneath the keel. Back on Salina you can pick a different anchorage from Day 3; if you tied up at Santa Marina before, try the buoy field at Rinella this time for the black sand and the sunset over Filicudi you just left behind. A short shore walk brings you to a couple of honest trattorias.
thingsToDo: Swim stop in deep water mid-channel
Photograph Filicudi's terraced north face
Anchor off Rinella's black-sand beach
Dinner at a Rinella trattoria
5.mooringTip: Rinella's small buoy field on Salina's south coast is calm in prevailing northwesterlies and handy for the beach, but exposed to any southerly swell. If the forecast looks unsettled, carry on to Santa Marina where the marina gives proper all-round shelter.

6.shortDescription: Sail to the smallest and most fashionable island for Bronze Age ruins above a turquoise cove and a famous sunset scene.
6.description: Panarea lies a few hours east, the tiniest of the seven and by some distance the most polished. Whitewashed houses, bougainvillea over the lanes, no cars to speak of, and a summer crowd that arrives by yacht. Pick up a buoy off the main village of San Pietro; the waters around the island are a protected marine reserve, so anchoring is restricted and the buoy fields are the sensible choice. The reward for the fees is Cala Junco, a near-perfect horseshoe cove on the southern shore with the Bronze Age village of Capo Milazzese on the headland above. Walk up in the late afternoon once the tour boats have gone. Come nightfall, the terrace at Hotel Raya has been the island's social anchor for decades, though the drinks cost roughly what the view is worth.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the horseshoe cove at Cala Junco
Walk to Capo Milazzese's Bronze Age village
Sundowner on the Hotel Raya terrace
Tender out to the Basiluzzo islet
6.mooringTip: Panarea sits inside a marine protected area, so use the official buoy fields off San Pietro rather than dropping anchor. They fill by early afternoon in season, so aim to arrive before lunch, and expect the swell to wrap in when the wind goes east.

7.shortDescription: Make the short passage to the ever-active volcano and time your arrival for the lava show down the Sciara del Fuoco after dark.
7.description: This is the day the whole route builds toward. Stromboli stands maybe twelve miles northeast of Panarea, a black cone that smokes without pause and has done for thousands of years. Sail up the east side to the roadstead off Scari, below the village of San Vincenzo, and go ashore for granita and a walk along the black-sand beach. The real event comes at dusk. Work the boat round to the northwest flank where the Sciara del Fuoco, the Stream of Fire, drops straight from the summit into the sea. Hold position a few hundred metres off and wait for the light to fade. Every twenty minutes or so the crater throws up glowing rock that tumbles down the scree, brighter as the sky darkens. Charter crews rarely forget their first night watching it.
thingsToDo: Watch the Sciara del Fuoco erupt at dusk
Walk San Vincenzo's black-sand beach
Granita stop in Stromboli village
Circle the Strombolicchio sea stack
7.mooringTip: The Scari roadstead is deep and open, fine for a lunch stop but rolly and unprotected for the night. Many crews view the Sciara after dark then run back to a buoy at Panarea to sleep; if you do stay off Stromboli, set plenty of scope and keep an anchor watch.

8.shortDescription: Ease back southwest to Panarea for the beach coves and clear-water swimming you sailed past on the way north.
8.description: After the drama of Stromboli, this is a gentler day. The hop back to Panarea takes only a couple of hours, so there is room to linger over breakfast and let the morning calm settle before the sea breeze builds. If you skipped it before, this is the day for the string of tiny islets scattered off Panarea's east coast, Lisca Bianca and Bottaro among them, where a shallow reef of volcanic gas bubbles up through the water and warms it in patches. Anchor the tender, mask on, and swim over the fizzing seabed. Back on the buoy at San Pietro, the evening runs slower than the crowds suggest. Grab an outside table in one of the lanes, order the caponata and a chilled Sicilian white, and watch the last tenders ferry people ashore.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the gas bubbles off Bottaro
Swim the shallows around Lisca Bianca
Browse San Pietro's boutique-lined lanes
Order caponata at a village trattoria
8.mooringTip: Use the San Pietro buoy fields again rather than anchoring inside the reserve. The eastern islets have day buoys for snorkelling stops; pick one up for a swim but do not rely on it overnight, as they sit open to the wind.

9.shortDescription: Head back to the capital for anything you missed first time, from thermal spa cliffs to the best granita on the islands.
9.description: The passage back to Lipari runs a comfortable ten miles or so southwest, an easy morning under sail with Salina off to starboard. Lipari deserves a second look, and coming back gives you the chance to do what the first stop was too short for. Take the boat round the west coast this time to the sea stacks at Pietra del Bagno, or push north to swim below the pumice cliffs where the white stone still slides into water so pale it looks lit from beneath. In town, the upper streets above the citadel stay quiet even in August. Find a spot on the ramparts at golden hour with a scoop of mulberry granita and watch the ferries come and go across to Vulcano. Provisioning is easy here too, so top up the boat before the run back to Sicily.
thingsToDo: Swim the sea stacks at Pietra del Bagno
Walk the quiet streets above the citadel
Sunset granita on the ramparts
Reprovision at the Lipari town shops
9.mooringTip: Come back into Marina Lunga for the better shelter and easier provisioning, or take a town buoy off Marina Corta if you want to be in the heart of things. In a strong northwesterly, Marina Corta gets uncomfortable, so the Pignataro side is the safer overnight bet.

10.shortDescription: A gentle final Aeolian leg back to Vulcano for one more mud soak and a last climb before turning for Sicily.
10.description: You can almost pick out Vulcano from the Lipari waterfront, and the crossing runs an hour at most, so this ranks as the most relaxed sail of the fortnight. There is no reason to hurry off the buoy. Motor across the narrow strait, past the Faraglione stack that guards the entrance, and settle back into Porto di Ponente for a second night. Round two at the mud baths is optional; some crews swear by it, others cannot face the smell again. Better, perhaps, to take the tender round to the base of the fumarole fields and snorkel where warm gas bubbles rise through the shallows and heat the water around your legs. Save the evening for the Ponente beach. This is the last night among the volcanoes before the long haul west to the Sicilian mainland, so it is worth a proper sunset and a decent bottle to mark the turn.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the warm fumarole shallows
Second soak in the Levante mud baths
Photograph the Faraglione entrance stack
Last volcano sunset from Porto di Ponente
10.mooringTip: Ponente does the job for one more night, tucked out of any easterly with the hook biting well into sand. Set the hook well and check it, because you want a settled night before an early start on the long Cefalù passage tomorrow.

11.shortDescription: The trip's big passage: some fifty miles across open sea to Cefalù's honey-stone cathedral and the great rock above it.
11.description: Today is the long one, roughly fifty miles of open Tyrrhenian back to the Sicilian coast, so treat it as a proper passage day. We leave Vulcano at first light and aim to have the anchor down at Cefalù before the afternoon breeze pipes up on the beam. Get the sails set early, keep a watch rotation going, and let the miles roll by. Cefalù comes into view from a long way out, the twin towers of its Norman cathedral standing against the huge crag of La Rocca that shelters the town. Ashore, the old lanes lead down to a medieval washhouse where spring water still runs to the sea, and the crescent of sand fills the moment the sun is out. After a fortnight of small island quays, a real Sicilian town feels like an event.
thingsToDo: Step inside the Norman cathedral's golden mosaics
Climb La Rocca for the rooftop view
Find the medieval Lavatoio washhouse
Swim off Cefalù's crescent town beach
11.mooringTip: Cefalù's old harbour is charming but open to the north and offers little real shelter, so most crews anchor off the beach in settled weather and watch the forecast closely. The small Presidiana marina just east has berths and services if the wind turns onshore; book ahead in summer.

12.shortDescription: Coast west to the Sicilian capital, mooring in the heart of the old city within reach of its markets and Norman splendour.
12.description: A shorter coastal run today, something like thirty-five miles west along the north shore with the mountains rising green to port the whole way. Palermo is a proper city and a jolt after the islands, loud and layered and endlessly worth it. La Cala marina sits right in the old town, so you can step off the boat and be among the market stalls of the Vucciria or Ballarò within minutes, where swordfish and blood oranges and street food crowd the lanes. Give the afternoon to the Cappella Palatina and the golden Byzantine ceilings above it. Eat where the locals queue, order the pane e panelle or the arancine, and finish with a cannolo filled to order. If you have an evening spare, the sweep of Mondello bay lies just west of the city and makes a fine anchorage.
thingsToDo: Graze the street food at Ballarò market
See the gold mosaics of the Cappella Palatina
Eat arancine where the locals queue
Swim in the bay at Mondello
12.mooringTip: La Cala marina puts you right in central Palermo, which is a huge convenience but books up fast in summer, so reserve well ahead. If it is full, Marina Villa Igiea just north is a smart alternative with good security and an easy taxi into the centre.

13.shortDescription: Turn back east along the coast toward Cefalù, with a lazy swim stop and one more evening under the great rock.
13.description: The eastbound run retraces yesterday's water, and with the prevailing northwesterly behind you it is usually a relaxed sail rather than a slog. Take your time. The coast between Palermo and Cefalù hides quiet coves worth a detour, so drop the hook somewhere off the cliffs near Sant'Ambrogio for lunch and a swim before pushing the last few miles east. Back at Cefalù you already know the ground, which is the pleasure of a second night. Skip the sights this time and just live in the place. Book a table in the old town for the evening, order the pasta with sardines and wild fennel that Sicily does better than anywhere, and watch the floodlit cathedral glow above the rooftops. Tomorrow is the short final leg home, so tonight is the one to let run late.
thingsToDo: Anchor for lunch off Sant'Ambrogio's cliffs
Eat pasta con le sarde in the old town
Evening stroll under the floodlit cathedral
Last swim off the town beach
13.mooringTip: Anchoring off the beach works again in fair weather; if the wind backs onshore, the Presidiana marina east of town is the bolt-hole. With only a short leg left tomorrow, pick whichever gives the calmer night and set the alarm early only if the forecast pushes you to.

14.shortDescription: The final run east back to Portorosa, closing a fortnight's loop with the Aeolians standing on the northern horizon.
14.description: The last leg carries you east along the Sicilian coast to the marina where it all began, a passage of forty-odd miles depending on how the wind sits. Leave Cefalù in good time; charter turnaround waits for nobody, and most bases want the boat back by late afternoon. On a clear day the whole chain you have just circled sits low on the horizon to the north, Vulcano and Lipari and the smoking cap of Stromboli, a fitting last look at where the fortnight was spent. Portorosa is a full-service marina with fuel, water and easy access, so build in time to refuel and pump out before you hand back the keys. Square away the boat, settle up, and let the crew take one more coffee on the quay while the two weeks sink in properly.
thingsToDo: Spot the whole Aeolian chain on the horizon
Refuel and pump out at Portorosa
Final crew coffee on the marina quay
Settle the boat for handover
14.mooringTip: Portorosa is a large, well-marked marina, but the approach channel is shallow in places, so follow the buoyed entrance and call ahead on the radio for a berth assignment. Leave a clear hour for fuelling and the pump-out station, which can queue on changeover days.

amalfi-cilento-14-days.metaDesc: Two weeks from Salerno: the Amalfi Coast, Naples and Ischia's thermal baths, then the wild Cilento caves at Palinuro before looping home to Salerno.

1.shortDescription: A short first hop out of Salerno to Cetara, the anchovy village where tuna boats still work the coast at dawn.
1.description: Most crews use this first leg to shake down the boat rather than cover ground. It is barely seven miles from Salerno, so there's no rush to leave, and provisioning in the city first makes sense. Cetara sits in a cleft below the coast road, still a genuine fishing port rather than a resort. Its boats land tuna and anchovies, and the village turns the latter into colatura di alici, an amber sauce descended from Roman garum. Order spaghetti dressed with it at one of the harbour trattorias and you'll understand the fuss. The port is small and shallow, built for local craft, so plan your night early. Walk up to the Torre Vicereale before dinner while the light drops. We usually keep the afternoon loose and let people find their sea legs.
thingsToDo: Taste spaghetti alla colatura at a harbour trattoria
Climb to the Torre Vicereale watchtower
Buy PDO colatura di alici to take home
Swim off the Marina di Cetara beach
1.mooringTip: Cetara's harbour is small and built for local fishing boats up to about 13 metres, with shallow water inside. Call ahead or arrive by early afternoon; larger yachts usually anchor off the beach in settled weather and tender in.

2.shortDescription: A gentle run west to Amalfi, where a striped cathedral rises above the quay and the old paper mills still turn.
2.description: Amalfi is close enough that you could leave after a long breakfast, though the anchorages fill by mid-afternoon in July. The town was a maritime republic a thousand years ago and it still trades on the sea. Marina Coppola takes yachts up to around thirty-five metres in eight to eleven metres of water, and there are moorings off the front, but southerly swell rolls straight in, so watch the forecast. Ashore, the Duomo di Sant'Andrea and its long staircase dominate the piazza. Duck into the Museo della Carta to see the water-driven paper presses that made the town rich. Buy lemons the size of fists and a bottle of limoncello. Evenings get busy, then day-trippers vanish by eight; that's the hour to eat.
thingsToDo: Climb the steps to Duomo di Sant'Andrea
Tour the water-powered Museo della Carta
Sample sfusato lemons and local limoncello
Walk the Valle delle Ferriere path inland
2.mooringTip: Marina Coppola handles yachts to about 35 metres in 8-11 metres, and there are town moorings off the seafront too. Southerlies send swell into the roadstead, so book a berth ahead in season and keep an eye on the wind backing south.

3.shortDescription: West past Praiano to Positano, the vertical town that photographs better from the water than from any street.
3.description: The stretch from Amalfi curls past Conca dei Marini and Praiano, worth a swim stop at the Grotta dello Smeraldo if the sea is flat. Positano itself has no harbour, only a jetty for landing. There are seasonal buoy fields off Spiaggia Grande and the quieter Fornillo beach, with a tender service running ashore. Depths drop away fast, and crews often struggle to anchor in under twenty metres while keeping clear of the swimming lines. Take a buoy if one's free and settle in. The town climbs the cliff in tiers of pink and ochre, and the walk up to Santa Maria Assunta with its majolica dome is short but steep. Eat at Fornillo for less noise. We generally aim to be moored by two.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the Grotta dello Smeraldo near Conca dei Marini
See the majolica dome of Santa Maria Assunta
Escape the crowds on Fornillo beach
Aperitivo above Spiaggia Grande at sunset
3.mooringTip: Positano has no marina, only buoy fields off Spiaggia Grande and Fornillo with a tender shuttle. Buoys are limited and the seabed shelves steeply, making anchoring awkward under 20 metres. Grab a mooring early or plan to move on if none are free.

4.shortDescription: Round Punta Campanella into the Bay of Naples and tie up at Sorrento's Marina Piccola under the cliffs.
4.description: This leg takes you around Punta Campanella, the tip of the peninsula, where the current can run and the wind funnels; time it for the morning calm. Off the point lie the Li Galli islets, said to be Homer's Sirens, good for a mid-passage swim. Sorrento sits on a tufa shelf above two marinas. Marina Piccola is the working port, sharing water with the Capri and Naples ferries, so expect wash and a busy quay. Marina Grande, further west, keeps its fishing-village feel and better restaurants. Up top, Piazza Tasso and the old lanes are made for an evening stroll. Order gnocchi alla sorrentina and a glass of the local limoncello. A lift or the zigzag ramp connects the harbour to the town.
thingsToDo: Swim off the Li Galli islets rounding the point
Wander Piazza Tasso and the old-town lanes
Dine on gnocchi alla sorrentina at Marina Grande
Ride the lift up from Marina Piccola
4.mooringTip: Marina Piccola is a functioning ferry port, so berths get wash and the quay is busy; book ahead in summer. For a calmer night with better tavernas, try Marina Grande to the west, though space there is tighter.

5.shortDescription: Cross the gulf to Naples and moor beneath Castel dell'Ovo in the Borgo Marinari, Vesuvius filling the skyline.
5.description: A proper crossing today, roughly eighteen miles of open gulf with Vesuvius growing off the bow. The sea breeze tends to build from late morning, so an early start earns you a flatter passage. Naples more than makes up for the early alarm. Tie up in the Borgo Marinari, the little harbour tucked against Castel dell'Ovo on the islet of Megaride, where fishermen and yachts to about twenty-four metres share the quay under the fortress walls. Step ashore and you're minutes from Santa Lucia and the seafront. This is the city for pizza in its birthplace, with the historic centre, Spaccanapoli, and the archaeological museum a taxi ride away. It's a loud, unvarnished place after the polished coast, and better for it. Lock the boat and go explore on foot.
thingsToDo: Eat a Margherita in its Naples birthplace
Tour Castel dell'Ovo above the harbour
Walk Spaccanapoli through the historic centre
See the bronzes at the Archaeological Museum
5.mooringTip: Borgo Marinari at Castel dell'Ovo berths yachts from about 10 to 24 metres with power and water on the quay. It sits in the middle of a working city, so confirm a berth in advance and leave nothing on deck when you head ashore.

6.shortDescription: Sail out to Ischia, the green volcanic island of vineyards and hot springs at the mouth of the gulf.
6.description: Ischia lies across the gulf from Naples, larger and greener than Capri and far less frantic. The passage is open water, so watch the afternoon breeze. Most crews make first for the Aragonese Castle, the fortress on its own islet linked to Ischia Ponte by a stone causeway; you can anchor off and dinghy in for a look. The island is volcanic through and through, which means thermal water everywhere and vineyards climbing the slopes for the local Biancolella white. Sant'Angelo, on the south side, is a car-free hamlet worth the detour if the weather allows a stop. Provisioning is easy in Ischia Porto. Ease off tonight before the north coast tomorrow. Swim, eat well, turn in early.
thingsToDo: Anchor off the Aragonese Castle at Ischia Ponte
Taste Biancolella white from the island's vineyards
Detour to car-free Sant'Angelo on the south coast
Soak in a natural thermal spring
6.mooringTip: Ischia Porto is a former volcanic crater with good shelter and full services, though it fills in high season. You can anchor off Ischia Ponte near the castle in settled weather, but it's exposed to any northerly, so keep the forecast close.

7.shortDescription: A short shift along Ischia's north coast to Casamicciola Terme, the island's old spa town below Monte Epomeo.
7.description: Barely an hour's sail moves you around to Casamicciola Terme, so treat today as a lazy one. Ischia's oldest spa resort has drawn people to its hot springs since the nineteenth century, and the marina here is modern and well run, taking yachts to around thirty-five metres inside the mole over a sandy bottom. The reason to come is the thermal parks. Parco Termale Castiglione stacks nine pools up the hillside at temperatures from thirty-two to forty degrees, with sea access below; it makes an easy afternoon. If you'd rather stay low-key, O'Vagnitiello is a smaller bathing park cut into the rock. Ashore, the rebuilt town is quieter than Ischia Porto. Book a spa slot, wander the front, and let the day drift. Tomorrow is a long one back across the gulf.
thingsToDo: Soak the day at Parco Termale Castiglione
Bathe in the rock pools of O'Vagnitiello
Ride up toward Monte Epomeo for the view
Stroll Casamicciola's rebuilt seafront promenade
7.mooringTip: Marina di Casamicciola is a sheltered, well-serviced port taking yachts to about 35 metres inside and larger craft alongside the outer quay, over sand in up to five metres. It's a comfortable, secure night, but reserve in July and August.

8.shortDescription: The long haul back across the Bay of Naples to Sorrento, Capri off to starboard the whole way.
8.description: This is the biggest single crossing of the trip so far, better started early while the gulf is still glassy. The rhumb line takes you south of Procida and across open water with Capri filling the view to starboard, tempting a detour if time and wind allow; the Faraglioni stacks alone are worth the diversion. Otherwise, hold for Sorrento and its shelf-top position above the sea. Coming back to Marina Piccola means a known quay and an easy walk into Piazza Tasso for a last coastal dinner. If you fancy a swim en route, the water off Punta Campanella is clear and deep. Fuel and provision here, because the run down to the Cilento starts tomorrow and the southern ports are smaller. Aim to be tied up before the afternoon breeze fills in.
thingsToDo: Detour to Capri's Faraglioni sea stacks
Swim in clear water off Punta Campanella
Provision and refuel at Marina Piccola
Last coastal dinner around Piazza Tasso
8.mooringTip: You already know Marina Piccola from earlier, so berthing is straightforward, but it stays a busy ferry port. Book ahead and, if you want quiet on this second visit, ask about Marina Grande instead.

9.shortDescription: Back around Punta Campanella to Amalfi for a second night, timed to beat the day-boats to a mooring.
9.description: Retracing the peninsula, you round Punta Campanella again and drop back onto the Amalfi Coast, this time from the west. It's a scenic fifteen miles or so past Positano, and there's no shame in a second swim below its cliffs if a buoy is going spare. Amalfi is the target for tonight, partly because it's a fine base and partly to reposition for the long jump to the Cilento tomorrow. Arriving mid-morning gives you the pick of the moorings before the tour boats swarm in. With the town already familiar, use the afternoon differently. Hike part of the Valle delle Ferriere up the gorge behind the town, past old ironworks and ferns, or take the bus up to Ravello for the gardens and the view from Villa Rufolo. Reprovision for the passage south.
thingsToDo: Hike the Valle delle Ferriere gorge above town
Bus up to Ravello's Villa Rufolo gardens
Second swim below the Positano cliffs
Reprovision for the Cilento crossing
9.mooringTip: Same story as your first Amalfi night: Marina Coppola berths and seafront moorings, both exposed to southerly swell. Get in before the day-boats to claim a spot, and reprovision now, because the Cilento ports south of here are smaller and quieter.

10.shortDescription: A long open crossing of the Gulf of Salerno to Agropoli, gateway to the Greek temples of Paestum.
10.description: Today the coast opens out and the crowds thin. It's a solid thirty-mile reach across the Gulf of Salerno to Agropoli, the northern gateway to the Cilento, so leave early and settle into the passage. The tourist port has floating pontoons and decent shelter, tucked in below the old town and its Aragonese castle, and it holds well against the sirocco. Agropoli feels like the real south after the polished Amalfi towns, a working place with a good fish market and honest trattorias. The main draw is inland. Paestum, half an hour away, keeps three Doric temples that have stood in the plain since the Greeks built them 2,500 years ago, among the best preserved anywhere. Arrange a taxi or hire bikes. Back aboard, the pace of the trip has clearly changed.
thingsToDo: Visit the Doric temples at Paestum
Climb to Agropoli's Aragonese castle and old town
Buy the day's catch at the fish market
Try mozzarella di bufala from the Sele plain
10.mooringTip: Agropoli's tourist port has floating pontoons and holds up well against the sirocco, unlike the exposed Amalfi roadsteads. It's calmer and cheaper here; a call ahead in peak weeks secures a finger berth, but pressure is lighter than up north.

11.shortDescription: South past Punta Licosa to Acciaroli, a low-key fishing harbour Hemingway is said to have loved.
11.description: A shorter, easier day rounds Punta Licosa, a wooded headland with a protected marine area and an islet off its tip, clear water over rock and posidonia that begs for an anchor-and-swim stop at midday. Acciaroli sits a little further south, a small fishing harbour with a genuine village behind it. Hemingway is rumoured to have passed through and drawn on the place for The Old Man and the Sea; true or not, it has the right weathered feel. The port takes yachts comfortably and stays relaxed. This is Cilento longevity country, where an unusual number of people live past a hundred, credited to the seafood, greens, and rosemary-heavy cooking. Eat simply and well by the water. There's little to do beyond swim, walk the mole, and watch the boats. That's the point.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim off Punta Licosa's marine reserve
Walk Acciaroli's harbour mole at dusk
Eat fresh seafood in the old village
Look for the Hemingway plaque by the port
11.mooringTip: Acciaroli's marina is small but welcoming and takes yachts without much fuss; it's a popular Cilento stop, so it can fill in August. The anchorage off Punta Licosa to the north is lovely by day but open, so use it for lunch, not overnight.

12.shortDescription: Down the wild Cilento shore to Cape Palinuro, honeycomb of sea caves and the famous Blue Grotto.
12.description: The coast turns dramatic today. Cape Palinuro juts out in a mass of cliffs and grottoes, more than thirty of them bored into the rock. The star is the Grotta Azzurra, where light through an underwater channel turns the water an electric blue; a local gozzo boat runs visits and you'll want to book, since only authorised craft may enter. Anchor for a swim in the Cala del Buondormire, the 'good sleep' bay under the headland, and look up at the Arco Naturale, a stone arch spanning the water. The little marina at Palinuro sits east of the cape and gets busy, so raft up early. Ashore it's a summer resort, cheerful and unpretentious. This is the turning point of the loop; from here the compass swings back north toward Salerno.
thingsToDo: Book a gozzo into the Grotta Azzurra
Anchor and swim in Cala del Buondormire
See the Arco Naturale stone arch
Explore the caves below Cape Palinuro
12.mooringTip: Palinuro's marina lies just east of the cape and is small and popular, so arrive early and expect to raft up in season. The Buondormire anchorage under the headland is fine for a daytime stop but too exposed to leave the boat overnight.

13.shortDescription: The long leg back north to Agropoli, retracing the Cilento coast with the wind on the beam.
13.description: A long one back up the Cilento, so top up fuel and water before you slip. The return north often gives a better sailing angle than the run down, with the prevailing breeze on the beam once it fills; use the early hours for miles and let the afternoon wind do the work. There's no rule saying you must push straight through. If the day's kind, break the passage at Marina di Camerota or drop the hook again off Punta Licosa for a last Cilento swim before the water gets busier further north. Agropoli makes a sensible night stop, a known, sheltered port within a day's reach of Salerno. Back in the tourist harbour, you'll notice how much quieter this end of the trip has been. A final fish dinner ashore feels earned.
thingsToDo: Break the passage at Marina di Camerota
Last Cilento swim off Punta Licosa
Refuel and reprovision at Agropoli's port
Final fish supper in the old town
13.mooringTip: You know Agropoli's tourist port from the way down, so berthing is easy and it stays well sheltered from the sirocco. Reserve if you're arriving late off a long leg, and fuel up here for the final run to Salerno.

14.shortDescription: The closing run across the gulf back to Salerno, the Amalfi cliffs rising ahead as the trip winds down.
14.description: The last leg closes the loop across the Gulf of Salerno, roughly thirty miles with the Amalfi wall growing ahead. Most charters want the boat back by a set time, so plan the morning around fuel state, the wind, and any final swim rather than sightseeing.
